Using Delightex, Year 4s will create 3D scenes where participants can explore historical and modern communication tools, encounter digital dilemmas, and make choices that affect the story's outcome. Students will script dialogue, embed quizzes, and build branching scenarios, then host their virtual workshops for younger classes, guiding them through the experience and reflecting on how technology shapes creative expression and safe communication.

Students use Minecraft Education to build a virtual wellbeing centre, designing interactive spaces that represent strategies for managing change, emotions, and relationships. Each area features NPCs or signs sharing tips and personal stories about thriving through transitions. Learners guide classmates through their centre, reflecting on how understanding themselves and others empowers them to thrive.

Students script and record their own podcasts exploring how technology transforms creative expression and communication. They interview peers or teachers about the evolution of communication tools and share their reflections on ethical and cybersafe digital practices, producing polished audio stories to present to younger students and the wider school community. This authentic action connects their learning to real-world communication and expression.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Project Ideation scenario and sets the bot's role as Leo, a student sustainability coach helping teams turn inquiry questions into realistic action projects. The topic is ?How We Express Ourselves: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.?. Students chat with the bot to test and refine possible inquiry actions or design ideas before committing to one plan; for example, they ask ?What small change could our class actually test this week?? and ?How will we know if our action helped??. After the chat, students produce a one-page project proposal naming the chosen idea, success criteria, resources, first prototype/action step and how it connects to the unit linked explicitly to How We Express Ourselves.

Students will create an interactive digital book that showcases the evolution of communication tools and creative expression, from ancient methods to modern digital platforms. Each student will research a key moment or invention in communication history, then use Book Creator to combine text, images (captured with iPad Camera), audio recordings, and even short video explanations. The final book will include a 'cybersafe tips' page, created collaboratively, and be published to share with younger students as part of their technology workshop. This hands-on project empowers students to become digital storytellers and ethical communicators, directly connecting their learning to authentic action in the school community.

Students work in teams to design and build their own interactive storytelling machines that blend technology and physical creativity. Using Sphero Indi robots, they craft a physical story map from recycled cardboard, clay, and craft materials, creating story 'scenes' and obstacles. Each group codes their Indi to travel through the map, triggering different story events or sound effects with coloured tiles and simple circuits (e.g., Makey Makey or battery-powered buzzers). Students prototype, test, and refine their machines using the Design Cycle, ensuring their story is engaging, interactive, and communicates a meaningful message about creative expression. The final machines are showcased to younger students, who can 'drive' the story themselves, connecting digital and physical forms of communication.

plannerText: This unit engages students in exploring how technology transforms creative expression and communication. Learners investigate the evolution of communication tools, ethical and cybersafe digital practices, and design and deliver technology workshops for younger students, culminating in authentic action that connects their learning to the wider school community.

ci: Systems connect us.
lo: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on communities

s.d: Students use the verified Minecraft lesson 'Filtering Water on the ISS' to explore how water filtration systems work as interconnected parts of a larger system. They build and test their own filtration models in Minecraft, then capture screenshots and write short reflections explaining how changes to components affect the system's function.

Students use Google Maps to identify and analyse real-world systems in their local community, such as transport networks, water flow, or school layouts. They create annotated maps highlighting connections, disruptions, and the impact of change, making abstract systems visible and tangible. This hands-on mapping empowers learners to see 'systems connect us' in action.

Students engineer chain reaction systems using Micro:bit, inspired by the 'Electrical Conductors' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=electrical-conductors-unit-of-work), to explore how systems connect us. By building and testing circuits, learners discover how inputs and outputs work together in both digital and physical systems, deepening their understanding of connections and interdependence. This interactive approach transforms abstract systems thinking into a tangible, collaborative experience.

Students engineer and code a Lego Spike Prime model to represent a key human system (such as transport, communication, or water supply) that connects people in a community. Teams design, build and program their system to demonstrate how different parts interact, then present their working model and explain how it mirrors real-world systems.

Learners design and code CoDrone EDU drones to navigate complex obstacle courses that represent interconnected systems, such as transportation or communication networks. By adjusting flight paths and programming responses to simulated disruptions, students experience firsthand how systems connect us and adapt to change. This engaging activity deepens understanding of systems thinking and real-world applications.

s.t: Systems in Motion: Rube Goldberg Chain Reaction
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, multi-step Rube Goldberg machine that models a real-world system (such as the water cycle, food chain, or school transport network). Using recycled materials, cardboard, marbles, dominos, and craft supplies, students physically construct each 'stage' of the system, ensuring each part connects and triggers the next. They integrate Sphero Indi robots as moving components?programming them via iPads to activate or respond to certain triggers (e.g., Indi drives onto a colored tile to knock over a lever or start a marble run). The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate system components, plan their chain reactions, prototype and refine their builds, and finally present how their machine demonstrates interconnectedness, change, and disruption within systems.

plannerText: This unit explores the central idea 'Systems connect us' through hands-on investigations of human-made and natural systems, including their parts, connections, and the impact of change or disruption. Students identify, map, and analyse real-world systems in their school and environment, conduct experiments (like the water cycle in a bag), and engage in collaborative projects to deepen their understanding of form, connection, and change within and across systems.

ci: Changes to the Earth impact environment and communities.
lo: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environments cause natural disasters; The impact of natural events on communities

s.d: Transform your students into 'Natural Disaster News Reporters'! In this innovative project, Year 4s will research a real-world natural disaster (such as bushfires, floods, earthquakes, or volcanic eruptions) and create a dynamic multimedia news report using Adobe Express. Students will combine images, video clips (recorded with the iPad Camera app), voiceovers, and text to explain the causes of the disaster, its impact on environments and communities, and how people responded and adapted. This engaging digital storytelling approach empowers students to synthesize their learning, practice digital literacy, and creatively communicate complex scientific ideas to an authentic audience.

s.t: Seesaw App Smash with iPad Camera
s.d: Have students document their hands-on STEM experiments (like the Earthquake Tower or Flood Simulation) using the iPad Camera app. They then upload annotated photos and video reflections to Seesaw, combining visual evidence with voice recordings to explain the science behind the disasters and how communities respond.

Students use Micro:bit to create animated models of volcanic eruptions, exploring geological changes and their impact on communities. They code sequences that simulate natural disasters, deepening their understanding of cause and effect in Earth's dynamic systems. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=volcano-animations-unit-of-work)

Students will become disaster engineers by designing and building earthquake simulators and flood-resistant structures using Lego Spike Prime. They'll test how different building techniques withstand simulated natural disasters, collect data on their designs' performance, and iterate to improve resilience. To showcase their findings, students will create dynamic presentations in Canva, combining photos, diagrams, and reflections to explain how communities can adapt to Earth's changing environments.

Students will explore how wastewater travels from homes to treatment plants, mirroring how communities respond to environmental challenges after natural disasters. In this hands-on Minecraft Education lesson, students investigate the journey of outflow, discover how biosolids are treated and recycled, and learn how clean water is returned to the environment. This directly connects to the unit's focus on how communities adapt to Earth's changes and manage environmental impacts. Students can map the wastewater system, identify potential disaster impacts (like floods or earthquakes), and propose improvements to make these systems more resilient. This immersive task empowers students to think like engineers and environmental scientists, building practical solutions for real-world problems.

Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively design, build, and test model communities that can withstand simulated natural disasters. First, they research real-world disasters (earthquakes, floods, bushfires) and brainstorm resilient features. Using physical materials like cardboard, popsticks, recycled plastics, clay, and fabric, they construct miniature buildings, bridges, and landscape features (e.g., levees, retaining walls). Integrating Sphero Indi robots or Bee-Bots, students code the robots to simulate the movement of floodwaters or seismic activity through their models. They observe how their structures respond, document the impact, and iterate their designs for improved resilience. Throughout, students capture their process with iPads (photos/videos), annotate their design choices, and present their final 'resilient community' prototypes to peers, reflecting on how technology and engineering can help communities adapt to Earth's changing environments.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 4 students in exploring how changes to the Earth, such as natural disasters, impact environments and communities. Through hands-on experiments, research projects, digital presentations, and case studies, students investigate the causes of geological change, the effects of extreme environments, and how communities respond and adapt to these challenges.

ci: Country, language and community are significant to the lives of First Nations people.
lo: The diversity of First Nations people; The importance of community, family and kinship; The importance of Country to First Nations people

s.d: Students will step into the role of storytellers and cultural custodians by exploring a mysterious shipwreck in Minecraft. They will research First Nations perspectives on Country, language, and community, then collaboratively build a digital narrative that weaves Dreaming stories, local Indigenous language, and the significance of place into their shipwreck adventure. Students will map out the shipwreck's surroundings, incorporate traditional symbols and art, and use in-game signs or books to share their reflections and creative interpretations. This immersive task empowers students to honour Indigenous voices and share their learning with the wider community in a dynamic, interactive way.

Students research and document diverse First Nations groups around the world, then collaboratively build a multimedia Padlet wall with images, audio, and video reflections. They can app smash by recording their personal reflections or interviews on Seesaw and embedding them into the Padlet, creating a living digital tapestry of perspectives and traditions.

Design a visual dictionary of Woiwurrung language words, pairing each word with student-created illustrations and context sentences. These can be printed for display or shared digitally, supporting the school's goal of exposing the community to Aboriginal language and fostering respect for linguistic diversity.

Produce interactive digital storybooks retelling Dreaming stories or exploring the significance of Country, language, and community. Students can include audio narration, original artwork, and embedded video to make the stories accessible and engaging for a wider audience.

Students produce a series of audio stories and interviews exploring the significance of Country, language, and community to First Nations people, using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com). They script, record, and edit their own podcasts to share Dreaming stories, language research, and reflections on Indigenous perspectives, culminating in a digital showcase for the school community. This authentic audio storytelling empowers students to amplify First Nations voices and deepen their understanding through creative communication.

Students work in teams to research and select key First Nations words, symbols, and stories connected to Country and community. Using recycled materials, clay, cardboard, and natural objects, they design and build a series of totem poles or 'story markers' that represent these ideas. Each totem incorporates a simple, student-built electronic sound module (Makey Makey or micro:bit with speakers) hidden inside, which plays a pre-recorded greeting, word, or short story in a local Indigenous language when touched. Students use iPads to record their audio and program the sound triggers. The completed Talking Totem Trail is set up as a physical path around the school grounds, inviting the community to interact with each totem, hear the language, and learn about its significance, blending digital storytelling with hands-on cultural making.

plannerText: This unit explores the significance of Country, language, and community to First Nations people, with students engaging in research, reflection, and creative activities such as mapping, language exploration, Dreaming stories, and artistic expression. Learning experiences include collaborative digital projects, visual and audio storytelling, and hands-on inquiry into Indigenous perspectives, culminating in authentic action and community sharing.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges and opportunities
lo: The reasons people migrate; Migration throughout history; Effects of migration on communities, cultures and individuals
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Leaders shape communities.
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; The purpose of leadership in communities; Qualities of influential leaders

s.d: Students collaboratively design an interactive 3D 'Leadership Gallery' using Delightex. Each student researches a real-world leader who has shaped a community, then creates a virtual exhibit featuring key facts, leadership qualities, and the leader's impact. Students add interactive elements such as quizzes, audio reflections, and branching scenarios where visitors make leadership decisions. The class hosts a virtual gallery walk, presenting their exhibits and reflecting on how diverse leadership styles shape communities.

Students design and publish digital 'Leadership Qualities' infographics or posters, highlighting the strengths, responsibilities, and impacts of leaders they research in the unit. These can be shared with the school community to inspire others and reinforce the unit's focus on leadership roles and community impact.

Learners design and distribute a digital survey using Microsoft Forms to gather opinions about leadership qualities from classmates. They analyse the results to identify which traits are most valued in leaders and discuss how these findings relate to real-world leadership in their community.

Guide students to collaboratively author a digital book titled 'Leaders in Our Community', combining research, interviews, and creative writing. Each student contributes a chapter profiling a leader (local, national, or global), explicitly linking their findings to the roles, responsibilities, and qualities explored in the unit.

Students launch a 'Leadership Voices' podcast, interviewing community leaders and reflecting on the qualities that shape effective leadership. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that explore how leadership impacts communities, fostering authentic connections and communication skills.

Students form small 'leadership teams' to design and physically build a miniature community using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies?featuring key spaces like a town hall, park, school, and homes. Each team programs a Sphero Indi robot to act as a 'leader' guiding citizens (represented by small figurines) through the community to solve challenges (e.g., organizing a clean-up, resolving a conflict, planning an event). Teams use the Design Cycle to empathize with community needs, ideate solutions, prototype their community layout and leadership pathways, test their robot's effectiveness as a leader, and refine both the physical build and coding. The project culminates in a showcase where teams explain how their robot's programmed 'leadership qualities' (e.g., clear communication, decision-making, empathy) helped shape and support their community.

plannerText: This unit explores the central idea 'Leaders shape communities' through inquiries into the roles and responsibilities of effective leadership, the purpose of leadership, and the strengths and qualities of a leader. Students engage in research, reflection, and creative activities to understand how leadership impacts communities, culminating in personal leadership applications and collaborative projects.

ci: People can create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences.
lo: The devices used to target audiences; Critical evaluation of messages; How we know we have been influenced

s.d: Transform your Year 5 students into media creators by having them design persuasive advertisements or campaign posters in Canva. Students critically analyse real-world media, then use Canva's powerful design tools?including the 'Remove Background' feature for their own photos (taken with the iPad Camera app)?to craft visually compelling messages targeting specific audiences. They'll experiment with colour, layout, and persuasive language, reflecting on how their choices influence beliefs and behaviours. This hands-on, creative process deepens their understanding of media manipulation and audience targeting, while building essential digital literacy and design skills.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?Revamp Melbourne? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/revamp-melbourne) to explore how communities express identity, values and belonging. They capture 2?3 screenshots or signs from the world and turn them into a labelled gallery or tour explaining the symbols, places or stories represented in the world.

Students create a podcast series where they interview community members or experts about how media messages have influenced their beliefs or actions. Using GarageBand, they edit and layer in sound effects or music to intentionally shape the listener's emotional response, then reflect on the ethics of audio manipulation.

Students curate a collaborative Padlet gallery of advertisements, song lyrics, and political speeches, then use Seesaw to record video reflections or critiques. They annotate examples with digital ink, highlighting persuasive devices and discussing how each message could be interpreted differently by various audiences.

Students will become media detectives by critically analysing real-world advertisements and mentor texts for persuasive devices and audience targeting. They will then create their own interactive persuasive media books in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio, and video (using the iPad Camera app) to craft messages aimed at specific audiences. Each student will reflect on their communication choices by adding voice recordings explaining how their techniques influence beliefs and behaviours. The final digital books can be published and shared for peer review, fostering discussion about the power and ethics of media manipulation.

Students work in teams to design and build physical 'message machines'?interactive arcade-style installations that deliver persuasive messages to a target audience. Using micro:bit (or Makey Makey) for simple inputs/outputs, students craft physical structures from cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each machine features buttons, levers, or sensors that trigger lights, sounds, or moving parts programmed to convey a persuasive message (e.g., environmental awareness, healthy eating). Students prototype, test, and refine both the physical build and digital code, reflecting on how design choices (visuals, interactivity, language, effects) influence audience response. The final arcade is set up for peers to experience and evaluate the impact of each machine's message.

plannerText: This unit explores how people create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences, focusing on critical evaluation of media, persuasive devices, and the impact of communication choices. Students analyse mentor texts, advertisements, and campaigns, create their own persuasive media, and reflect on how messages influence beliefs and behaviours.

ci: Entrepreneurs have a responsibility to develop, market and sell sustainable products and services.
lo: The power of giving back; How our environment is impacted by the products we create; Sustainable business practices

s.d: Students will use Adobe Express to create dynamic, interactive marketing materials for their sustainable products or services. They can design eye-catching digital posters, infographics, and short promo videos that highlight their product's unique features, sustainability benefits, and environmental impact. These digital assets can be displayed at the market stall event via QR codes or on iPads, allowing students to practice real-world digital marketing skills and reflect on how entrepreneurs use media to influence consumer choices for a more sustainable future.

Create a digital marketing campaign for their sustainable product, including social media posts, infographics, and animated advertisements. Students use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create professional product images and app smash with iPad Camera for original photography.

Develop and distribute a market research survey to the school community to gather data on consumer preferences and sustainability awareness. Analyse the results in Excel to inform product design and marketing strategies.

Collaboratively build a digital 'Entrepreneurial Journey Wall' where students document each stage of their business development process, share reflections, and receive peer feedback. Include embedded videos, sketches, and pitch rehearsals.

Students interview local entrepreneurs or classmates about how they develop and market sustainable products, then use Adobe Express Podcasting to record and edit their audio stories. They create a podcast episode that highlights responsible business practices and reflects on the environmental impact of consumer choices, sharing their learning with the school community.

Students work in teams to design and construct a physical miniature market stall from recycled materials (cardboard, fabric scraps, bottle caps, etc.), representing their sustainable product or service. They integrate a simple programmable microcontroller (e.g., Micro:bit or Makey Makey) to add interactive digital features?such as touch-activated customer feedback buttons, LED-lit product displays, or motion-triggered sound effects. Each stall must physically showcase their product prototype, include hand-crafted signage, and use the embedded tech to collect data or enhance the customer experience. The project follows the Design Cycle: empathize with customers, define needs, ideate stall features, prototype and test both physical and digital elements, and refine based on peer feedback before the final market event.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 5 students inquire into the responsibilities of entrepreneurs to develop, market, and sell sustainable products and services. They engage in market research, apply the design thinking process, prototype and test their ideas, and culminate in a market stall event where they pitch and sell their creations, reflecting on the impact of consumer choices and business practices on the environment.

ci: Design processes enable people to respond creatively to challenges in the natural world.
lo: The nature and impact of natural hazards (Causation); How design processes are used to generate and test solutions (Function); The ethical and practical considerations in human responses to natural challenges (Responsibility)

s.d: Students design interactive 3D models of natural hazards using Merge Cube and bring them to life with Delightex, allowing peers to explore and annotate features such as volcanoes, earthquakes, or flood zones. This immersive app smash deepens understanding of how design processes respond to challenges in the natural world, as learners manipulate and present their models in augmented reality. The experience culminates in a virtual showcase where students explain their creative solutions and scientific thinking.

Teams harness Google Maps to investigate natural hazards around the world, pinpointing earthquake zones, floodplains, and bushfire-prone areas. They overlay historical data and recent events, drawing connections between geography and human response. This real-world mapping fuels authentic inquiry into how design processes address environmental challenges.

Students will use Micro:bits to code dynamic volcano animations, simulating eruptions and seismic activity. In small groups, students research a real-world volcanic hazard, then design and program their Micro:bit to visually represent eruption sequences, lava flow, or warning signals. They test and debug their animations, then present their coded volcano models alongside data about the natural hazard and human responses. This hands-on task brings natural hazards to life and deepens understanding of disaster warning systems and the science behind eruptions.

Learners design and build a Lego Spike Prime structure that can withstand simulated natural hazards, such as earthquakes or floods, linking to their study of design processes and natural disasters. They test and refine their models, documenting the design changes and presenting their findings on what makes a structure resilient.

Students use CoDrone EDU to prototype and test aerial solutions for disaster response, such as delivering supplies or surveying affected areas. Teams code drones to navigate model landscapes, reflecting on how design processes enable creative responses to natural challenges. This immersive activity connects engineering, geography, and humanitarian action in a memorable way.

Students work in teams to investigate a specific natural hazard (e.g., earthquake, flood, bushfire). Using the Design Cycle, they sketch, plan, and physically construct a sturdy mini-home prototype from materials like cardboard, popsticks, recycled plastics, fabric, and clay. Teams embed a Sphero Indi or similar programmable robot inside their home to simulate a 'family' that must remain safe during hazard simulations. Students code the robot to react to hazard scenarios (e.g., shaking for earthquakes, moving to higher ground for floods), while testing and refining their physical designs against teacher-created 'hazard events' (e.g., shaking table, water spray, wind tunnel). The project culminates in a showcase where students pitch their disaster-resilient homes, demonstrating both their physical engineering and digital problem-solving.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in the design thinking process as they investigate natural hazards and human responses. Through provocations, data analysis, mapping, and hands-on prototyping, students develop and test creative solutions to real-world challenges, culminating in persuasive pitches and a community showcase.

ci: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.
lo: Types of conflict; Causes and impacts of conflict; Conflict resolution

s.d: Learners create 3D conflict scenario dioramas on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate peaceful resolutions and dialogue between characters. This app smash empowers students to visualise and role-play conflict resolution strategies, making abstract concepts tangible as they explore causes, impacts, and solutions to conflict in local and global contexts. The interactive models spark rich discussion and reflection on how peaceful actions can change the world.

Students design and distribute a class survey using Microsoft Forms to collect peers' opinions on different types of conflict and effective peaceful resolutions. They analyse the collected data to identify common causes and impacts of conflict, then collaboratively create a summary report that highlights key findings and suggests strategies for conflict resolution aligned with the unit?s central idea.

Students create a 'Peace Talks' podcast, analysing protest songs, historical events, and personal stories of conflict resolution. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they collaborate to script and record episodes that explore peaceful solutions to conflict, amplifying diverse perspectives and fostering empathy.

Students collaborate to build a Lego Spike Prime conflict resolution machine?an interactive model that demonstrates peaceful solutions to conflict scenarios. They code the model to show different outcomes based on choices, then share a reflection on how technology can help visualise and solve real-world disagreements.

Students will step into the shoes of Nobel Peace Laureates in Minecraft: Active Citizen, exploring real stories of individuals who resolved conflicts and promoted peace around the world. In small groups, students will investigate a Laureate's journey, interact with in-game scenarios that highlight different types and causes of conflict, and collaboratively build digital monuments or peace gardens that symbolise peaceful conflict resolution. After their Minecraft experience, students will use Canva or Adobe Express to create a visual presentation or digital story, reflecting on the strategies used by these global citizens and proposing how similar approaches could be applied to conflicts in their own school or community.

s.t: Peace Pathways: Interactive Conflict Resolution Board
s.d: Students collaboratively design and build a large, physical board game that simulates real-world conflict scenarios and peaceful resolution strategies. Using cardboard, recycled materials, clay, and art supplies, they craft the game board, event cards, and player pieces representing diverse cultures and perspectives. Integrating Sphero Indi robots or similar, students program these robots to act as 'peacemakers' that move along the board based on players' decisions and outcomes. As part of the Design Cycle, students empathise by researching global and local conflicts, define key issues, ideate peaceful solutions, prototype their board and tech integrations, test play with classmates, and refine both physical and digital elements. This hands-on, hybrid project fosters creativity, critical thinking, and empathy by blending tangible making with interactive technology.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 5 students in exploring types, causes, and impacts of conflict, with a strong focus on peaceful conflict resolution both locally and globally. Learning experiences include analysing protest songs, picture books, and historical events, as well as creating collaborative digital resources and participating in practical conflict resolution activities.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has shaped society.
lo: The impact of colonisation; Colonisation stories; The effects of colonisation ??? past, present and future

s.d: Transform your exploration of colonisation and its impacts by having students create immersive, interactive 3D scenes using Delightex. Students can collaboratively build virtual environments that represent key moments in Australian colonisation from both Indigenous and colonial perspectives. For example, they might design a traditional Indigenous landscape, then overlay changes brought by European settlement, visually demonstrating the effects on people and places. Students can embed historical sources, creative writing, and voiceovers to tell personal stories of colonisation, fostering empathy and critical thinking. Presentations can be shared with the class, allowing for rich discussion and analysis of multiple viewpoints.

Students use Google Maps to trace the spread of colonisation, mapping key journeys, settlements, and the impact on Indigenous lands. By comparing historical and current maps, they visualise changes to place and society, sparking critical conversations about legacy and perspective. This mapping activity brings the story of colonisation to life in a dynamic, visual way.

Transform your exploration of colonisation into a dynamic, interactive timeline or digital storytelling project using Canva. Students will collaborate to create visually engaging presentations that weave together Indigenous and colonial perspectives, historical maps, primary sources, and creative writing. By designing infographics, storyboards, and empathy-driven character profiles, students will critically analyse the impacts of colonisation on people and places, bringing history to life through powerful visuals and thoughtful design. This approach fosters deep understanding, creativity, and empathy, making the past relevant and meaningful for today's learners.

Learners construct a Lego Spike Prime timeline machine that physically moves through key events of colonisation, using sensors or motors to trigger short presentations or facts at each stop. This hands-on build helps students visualise the sequence and impact of historical events, culminating in a group reflection on the journey.

Students will create an interactive digital timeline in Adobe Express, showcasing key events and personal stories from both Indigenous and colonial perspectives during the colonisation of Australia. Each student will research a specific event or figure, then design a timeline entry that includes historical images (using the iPad Camera app for any original artwork or models), primary source quotes, and their own creative writing to represent multiple viewpoints. The timeline will be published as a class web page, allowing students to explore and reflect on how colonisation has shaped society, fostering empathy and critical thinking about the past, present, and future.

Students work in teams to design and build interactive 'time machine' dioramas that physically depict a key moment or place from both Indigenous and colonial perspectives. Using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and paint, each diorama features movable elements (e.g., sliding doors, pop-up figures, rotating scenery) to show change over time. Students embed simple circuits (LEDs, buzzers) powered by Makey Makey or similar kits to highlight areas or trigger recorded audio snippets (using iPads and conductive materials) that share first-person stories or historical facts. The physical build and tech components are fully integrated: for example, touching a clay figure of a settler or Indigenous person plays their story, or opening a flap reveals contrasting environmental impacts. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathise by researching diverse perspectives, define a moment to portray, ideate interactive features, prototype and test their builds, and reflect on how their diorama communicates the complexities of colonisation.

plannerText: This unit explores how colonisation has shaped society by examining its impacts, sharing colonisation stories, and analysing effects on people and places from multiple perspectives. Students engage with historical sources, creative writing, mapping, and hands-on projects to understand both Indigenous and colonial viewpoints, fostering empathy and critical thinking about the past, present, and future.

ci: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.
lo: How values and beliefs impact choices; How people's choices and actions impact others; Responsible decision-making

s.d: Students will create an interactive digital poster or infographic in Canva that explores how personal beliefs and values shape decision-making and the ripple effects of those choices on others. They will interview classmates or family members about a value-driven choice, then visually map out the perspectives, motivations, and impacts using Canva's design tools. This creative process encourages empathy, critical thinking, and collaboration, while allowing students to reflect on their own values and how their actions influence their community and the world.

Students design and code a Lego Spike Prime 'Decision Machine' that models how beliefs and values influence choices and actions. By programming different pathways and outcomes, learners explore the consequences of various decisions, then present their machine and discuss how personal values shape real-life outcomes.

Students will create an interactive digital story using Adobe Express, exploring a real-life scenario where personal beliefs and values influence decision-making. Each student will design a 'Ripple Effect' visual journey: starting with a single choice, they will map out the cascading impacts on individuals, communities, and even global contexts. By integrating images, text, and voice narration, students will reflect on how their own values shape their actions and how those actions can inspire positive change. This project encourages empathy, critical thinking, and creativity, making the abstract concept of 'Who We Are' tangible and memorable.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Character Interview scenario and sets the bot's role as Ava, a community celebration organiser explaining why particular traditions matter to different families. The topic is ?Who We Are: People's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values and can impact others.?. Students chat with the bot to explore a named perspective through guided questioning rather than general research; for example, they ask ?How does this celebration show what your community values?? and ?What might outsiders misunderstand about this tradition??. After the chat, students produce a question bank plus a short perspective-and-evidence summary that compares the chatbot responses with unit sources linked explicitly to Who We Are.

Students will collaboratively design immersive 3D scenarios that explore how personal beliefs and values shape choices and ripple out to impact others. In small groups, students create branching decision pathways: each path represents a different value-driven choice in a real-world scenario (e.g., standing up for a friend, responding to a community issue, or making an ethical purchase). As they build, students must consider and visually represent the consequences of each decision, showing both positive and negative ripple effects on individuals and communities. The final interactive 3D experiences are shared with peers, who 'walk through' each scenario, reflect on perspectives, and discuss responsible decision-making.

s.t: Values in Motion: Chain Reaction Empathy Machine
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large Rube Goldberg-style chain reaction machine that physically represents how one person's choices and values can create ripple effects in a community. Using recycled materials (cardboard, tubes, marbles, dominoes, string, etc.), students construct interconnected segments, each symbolizing a belief, value, or action (e.g., kindness, honesty, sharing). At key points, teams integrate simple programmable robotics (such as Sphero Indi or Bee-Bots) to trigger or be triggered by physical mechanisms?bridging digital and physical worlds. The machine is mapped and documented digitally (using iPads for video, time-lapse, or simple animation overlays) to narrate the journey of an 'action' through the system. Students reflect on each segment's impact, fostering discussion on how values-driven decisions can set off positive or negative chains in real life. The project follows the Design Cycle: Inquire (research values and chain reactions), Develop Ideas (plan machine segments), Create (build and integrate tech), and Evaluate (test, reflect, iterate).

plannerText: This unit explores how people's choices are influenced by their beliefs and values, and how these choices can impact others. Students engage in reflective, creative, and collaborative activities to examine perspectives, responsible decision-making, and the ripple effects of actions within local and global contexts.

ci: An inquiry into the ways in which we discover and express ideas, feelings, nature, culture, beliefs and values.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Student-led exhibition (PYPEX) ??? Students develop and present their own units of inquiry.
lo: Student-led transdisciplinary themes; Approaches to learning; Research and presentation skills

s.d: Transform your Year 6 students into political campaigners and government storytellers! Using Adobe Express, students will create dynamic, interactive presentations or infographics that explain Australia's political system, the journey of a bill becoming law, and compare global government structures. They can embed video interviews from their mock debates, design campaign posters, and even craft animated timelines of key historical changes in governance. This creative approach empowers students to synthesise research, role-play experiences, and critical reflections into visually compelling digital artefacts?perfect for sharing with peers and the wider school community.

The teacher creates a Wise Discussion Chatbot using the Project Ideation scenario and sets the bot's role as Kai, a project coach who helps students narrow broad inquiry ideas into one practical proposal. The topic is ?How We Organise Ourselves: Student-led exhibition (PYPEX) ??? Students develop and present their own units of inquiry.?. Students chat with the bot to test and refine possible inquiry actions or design ideas before committing to one plan; for example, they ask ?Which idea best connects to our central idea?? and ?What resources, success criteria and next steps do you need??. After the chat, students produce a one-page project proposal naming the chosen idea, success criteria, resources, first prototype/action step and how it connects to the unit linked explicitly to How We Organise Ourselves.

Students design persuasive campaign posters and infographics for their invented political parties, using Canva's templates and 'Remove Background' feature to create visually compelling materials for the mock election and preferential voting activities.

Exhibition groups use Lego Spike Prime to prototype a moving model or interactive display that represents their PYPX inquiry theme. Students integrate sensors, motors, and creative coding to bring their research to life, then showcase their model as part of their exhibition presentation.

Students work in collaborative groups to research and compare Australia's political system with another country's government. Each group creates an interactive digital book that explains the structure, law-making process, and key values of both systems. They embed student-recorded audio debates, annotated diagrams of parliament, and short video reflections using the iPad Camera app. The final Book Creator publication is shared with the class for peer review, fostering critical discussion about how different systems shape civic participation.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large-scale, physical maze representing the journey of a bill through Australia's parliament, using recycled cardboard, popsticks, and craft materials for chambers, committees, and key decision points. They then program a Sphero Indi or similar robot (decorated as a 'bill') to navigate the maze, triggering interactive checkpoints (e.g., QR codes or simple circuits with Makey Makey) that require students to role-play debates, amendments, or votes before progressing. This hybrid project immerses students in the legislative process by combining digital coding, hands-on building, and real-time collaborative problem-solving, all guided by the Design Cycle.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 6 students in the structure and function of Australia's political system, the processes of law-making, and comparative global government systems. Through mock elections, debates, role-plays, research, and creative projects, students critically examine how values and structures shape governance and civic participation.

ci: Energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress.
lo: Different forms of energy and how they are used; The impact of different energy sources on the planet; Sustainable energy practices

s.d: Teams build virtual energy systems?like wind farms or solar grids?on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to simulate energy transformations and track their impact on human progress. This hands-on app smash lets students experiment with converting and storing energy, visualising invisible processes in 3D, and presenting their findings in an engaging, interactive format. The activity brings the science of energy to life and encourages creative problem-solving for sustainable futures.

Students use Micro:bit to gather real-time data about energy use and experiment with simple data logging, directly connecting to the unit's focus on energy transformation and sustainability. Learners design investigations to measure light, temperature, or movement, then analyse their findings to propose more sustainable energy practices. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=energy-awareness)

Students use the verified lesson 'Alternative Energy' to explore how hydropower, wind, and waste-to-energy systems generate power and impact the environment. They create models of energy plants in Minecraft and document their designs with signs explaining energy conversion and sustainability.

Students document their energy investigations by creating interactive digital books in Book Creator. Each student or group can include photos of their experiments (using the iPad camera), diagrams, voice recordings explaining energy conversions, and reflections on sustainable practices, resulting in a multimedia portfolio.

Transform your energy unit by challenging students to design and code Sphero BOLT robots to demonstrate real-world energy transformations! Students will investigate how electrical energy from the BOLT's battery is converted into kinetic energy as it moves, and experiment with variables like speed, direction, and obstacle navigation. They'll create interactive 'energy relay' courses, simulating how energy is transferred and transformed in everyday systems. To showcase their learning, students will collaboratively document their design process, code, and scientific explanations in a visually engaging Canva presentation, making connections to sustainable energy practices and the engineering design cycle.

Students work in teams to design and build large, interactive kinetic sculptures that visually demonstrate energy transformation (e.g., solar, wind, or mechanical to motion or light). Using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, students construct moving parts such as gears, levers, and wheels. They then integrate simple robotics kits (e.g., Sphero Indi or basic motors controlled by iPads) to power their sculptures using renewable energy sources (like solar panels or hand-cranks). Students prototype, test, and refine their sculptures through the Design Cycle, documenting their process digitally. The final creations are displayed in a 'Sustainable Energy Gallery Walk' where students explain the energy transformations their sculpture demonstrates and reflect on real-world applications.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 6 students in hands-on and inquiry-driven experiences exploring how energy is converted, transformed, and used to support human progress. Activities include investigating diverse energy sources, analysing their impacts, constructing circuits, building Solar Buddy lights, and reflecting on sustainable practices through collaborative projects and real-world applications.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for sustainable practices; Taking action for environmental stewardship

s.d: Students will explore a sustainable forest in Minecraft to understand the balance between conservation, community needs, and environmental stewardship. In this immersive task, students investigate how sustainable forestry practices protect both wildlife and human interests. They will identify and analyse the social, economic, and environmental impacts of different management strategies, then collaborate to design their own sustainable forest area within the game. Afterward, students will use Canva to create a visually compelling advocacy campaign, presenting their forest management plan and persuasive messages to inspire real-world conservation action.

Students collaboratively document and compare environmental stewardship actions from Camp Mallana and their own communities on a shared Padlet. They then use Canva to design digital posters or infographics advocating for a specific conservation action, integrating real reflections and data from their experiences.

Students create and record a podcast series using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com) that explores how different communities interact with the environment. They produce audio episodes featuring stories from their Camp Mallana experiences, Indigenous land management practices, and their own conservation action projects, highlighting community values and sustainable stewardship. This task deepens understanding of the unit?s central idea by encouraging students to communicate how environmental management reflects community priorities and inspires action.

Students will create a visually compelling digital advocacy campaign using Canva, focusing on an environmental issue explored during Camp Mallana or Indigenous land management case studies. In teams, students will research a local conservation challenge, then design a series of posters, infographics, and social media graphics that educate the community and inspire action. Each group will use Canva's collaborative features to combine Indigenous perspectives, scientific facts, and creative calls-to-action, culminating in a digital exhibition to drive real-world stewardship.

Students engineer a Lego Spike Prime environmental stewardship robot that demonstrates a sustainable action?such as sorting recyclables or simulating tree planting. They code and test their robot, then create a short video or poster explaining how their design supports community interaction with the environment.

Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, multi-layered diorama showcasing a local ecosystem under threat (e.g., wetlands, bushland, or coastal area). Using recycled materials, natural objects, cardboard, and clay, students physically construct habitats, native flora, and fauna. Embedded within the diorama are simple circuits (LEDs, buzzers, or motors powered by Makey Makey or microcontrollers like Arduino Uno) to represent environmental changes or human impacts?such as lighting up areas affected by pollution, or triggering movement to show animal migration. Students program interactive touchpoints (e.g., conductive paths with copper tape) so that when visitors touch specific features, audio clips or digital fact cards (triggered via connected iPads or laptops) share Indigenous land management strategies or conservation actions. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize (research local issues), define (choose a focus), ideate (sketch and plan), prototype (build and code), test (peer feedback), and refine, culminating in a hands-on exhibition advocating for stewardship and creative change.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in environmental stewardship through Camp Mallana experiences, Indigenous land management case studies, and action projects focused on conservation. Students explore how community values shape environmental management, the need for conservation, and ways stewardship can drive change, culminating in advocacy and creative action.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Significant events shape a place
lo: What is a significant event; Why these events occurred; Impact of significant events

s.d: Students reconstruct significant historical events as interactive 3D scenes on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to add narration, animations, and primary source overlays. This app smash allows learners to step inside moments that shaped places and societies, fostering empathy and critical thinking as they explore cause and effect. The resulting digital artefacts serve as powerful conversation starters for class discussions and personal inquiries.

Learners dive into Google Maps to investigate how significant events?like migrations or discoveries?have shaped places over time. They create interactive timelines by dropping pins on key locations, adding images and stories to each, and presenting their findings as a digital journey through history. This approach makes the interconnectedness of events and places tangible and engaging.

Students will create an interactive digital timeline in Canva that showcases a significant migration, discovery, or historical milestone of their choice. Each student will research the causes, impacts, and personal stories connected to their chosen event, then design a visually engaging timeline with embedded images, maps, and short video or audio reflections (recorded using the iPad Camera app). The timeline will highlight how individuals and groups were interconnected and how the event shaped societies over time. This creative project encourages students to synthesise historical research, personal inquiry, and digital storytelling, culminating in a dynamic presentation that brings history to life.

Learners build a Lego Spike Prime model that represents a significant event in local or global history, using movement or sensors to tell the story of change and impact. They present their interactive model alongside a timeline or storyboard, making connections between engineering and historical understanding.

Students explore the verified Minecraft lesson 'Historical Homes' to investigate how significant events shaped societies and architecture. They create annotated builds of homes or community structures that reflect the social and cultural impacts of their chosen event, capturing screenshots and writing reflections to share their understanding.

Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, interactive board game that simulates the journey of individuals or groups during a significant migration, discovery, or historical milestone. Using recycled cardboard, clay, fabric scraps, and art supplies, they craft the game board with physical 3D terrain, landmarks, and obstacles representing real-world challenges. Students integrate Sphero Indi robots as 'player pieces' that move across the board, programmed via iPads to respond to color-coded tiles and sensors representing decision points, events, or cultural encounters. Throughout the Design Cycle, students research their chosen event, prototype physical components, iterate rules and mechanics, and code Sphero Indis to interact with the board, culminating in a playable experience that demonstrates the interconnectedness and impact of historical journeys on societies and places.

plannerText: In this unit, students investigate how significant events?such as migrations, discoveries, or historical milestones?shape places and societies. They explore personal and global histories, analyse the causes and impacts of these events, and conduct personal inquiries into events of interest, culminating in informative reports and creative presentations that demonstrate their understanding of the interconnectedness of individuals, groups, and significant events.

ci: Personal changes and influences contribute to our evolving sense of self.
lo: Physical, social, emotional and intellectual changes that occur throughout life; Influences that shape our identity; Developing a sense of self

s.d: Learners design digital 'identity cubes' on Merge Cube, layering personal influences, values, and milestones in 3D, then animate their evolving sense of self with Delightex. This app smash transforms abstract reflections into interactive artefacts, letting students visually map how personal changes and external influences shape who they are. The process encourages deep self-reflection and creative storytelling, culminating in a gallery walk of digital identities.

Students record and produce personal podcasts exploring how physical, social, emotional, and intellectual changes have shaped their evolving sense of self. They script and share stories about key influences in their lives, using Adobe Express Podcasting?s editing tools to add sound effects and music that enhance their reflections.

Students will create an interactive 'Digital Identity Evolution' timeline using Adobe Express. They will visually map out key personal changes, external influences, and pivotal moments that have shaped their sense of self over time. Each timeline entry can include images, icons, and short reflections, allowing students to creatively showcase how their identity has developed. The project encourages deep personal inquiry, digital storytelling, and peer feedback, culminating in a class gallery walk where students can comment on each other's evolving journeys.

Students create a Lego Spike Prime 'Identity Machine' that uses coded movements or displays to represent the influences and changes that shape personal identity. They share their model with peers, explaining how each programmed feature symbolises a different aspect of their evolving sense of self.

Students collaboratively build a dynamic 'Influences on Me' wall. Each student posts digital identity maps, stories, or reflections about personal changes and external influences shaping who they are. Peers comment with thoughtful feedback, questions, or connections, creating a living tapestry of evolving identities. Teachers can prompt with guiding questions, and students can update their posts over time to show growth and shifting perspectives.

Students design and build a physical 'time capsule' sculpture that visually represents their evolving identity, using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies. Embedded within each capsule is a simple programmable robot (e.g., Sphero Indi or similar), which students code to perform a movement or light sequence that symbolises an aspect of their personal journey or influences. The robot is physically integrated?hidden or revealed through a crafted mechanism (e.g., a door, drawer, or rotating base)?and triggers when peers interact with the sculpture. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate personal identity themes, plan capsule designs, prototype with materials, program the robot's sequence, test mechanisms, and present how both the physical and digital elements express 'Who We Are'. This merges hands-on building, coding, and reflective storytelling in a unique, tangible artefact.

plannerText: The unit 'Who We Are' explores how personal changes and external influences contribute to students' evolving sense of self. Key activities include digital identity mapping, collaborative reflection on influences, creative storytelling, and inquiry into how perceptions are shaped over time. The unit is highly transdisciplinary, connecting personal, social, and emotional learning with digital creation and peer feedback.

ci: Emotions influence our wellbeing.
lo: How our feelings and emotions affect us; How feelings and emotions are interpreted; How our emotions and behaviour affect relationships

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to create an 'emotion maze,' programming the car to travel through different zones that represent feelings like happiness, sadness, or excitement. As Indi moves, students share stories or strategies for managing each emotion, making abstract wellbeing concepts hands-on and engaging.

Students take photos of their Colour Monster artwork and use ChatterPix Kids to record their voices describing the emotion their monster represents, sharing a time they felt that way. This empowers students to express feelings in their own words and supports oral language development.

Students use Word Clouds ABCya to collect and visualise words that describe different emotions and their effects on wellbeing. The resulting word cloud sparks discussion about which feelings are most common and how they influence our relationships.

Students create individual digital collages using PicCollage to express different emotions connected to the unit's central idea. They combine drawings, photos, emojis, and simple words or phrases to show how feelings influence their wellbeing and relationships, then share their collages in a class slideshow to discuss and reflect on emotional expression.

Students create simple puppet shows using Puppet Pals to act out scenarios where characters experience different emotions and demonstrate ways to manage those feelings. Through storytelling and role-play, learners explore how emotions influence behaviour and relationships, then share their digital puppet performances with the class to reflect on wellbeing and empathy.

Students work in teams to design and build hand or stick puppets representing different emotions using felt, recycled fabric, cardboard tubes, and craft materials. They also collaboratively construct a simple puppet stage from boxes and decorate it with emotion-themed backgrounds. Using iPads, students record short audio clips expressing different feelings (e.g., happy, sad, excited, worried) in their own voices. Each puppet is fitted with a small, color-coded button (made from cardboard and colored paper) that, when pressed, triggers the corresponding audio clip via a simple Bluetooth speaker (with teacher support). The class then uses their puppets and stage to perform short skits about managing emotions, blending creative making, digital storytelling, and hands-on building to deepen emotional understanding.

plannerText: This Prep HWEO unit explores how emotions influence wellbeing, with students learning to identify, interpret, and manage their feelings through activities like Colour Monster art, emotion discussions, and reflection on how behaviour affects relationships. The unit integrates visual arts, literature, and music to help students express and understand emotions, culminating in creative and collaborative tasks that support emotional intelligence and positive classroom culture.

ci: The products people use go through a process of change.
lo: Where food comes from; How food can be changed for different purposes; An appreciation of food

s.d: Learners film and edit short documentaries in iMovie, capturing the journey of food from seed to table. They combine video clips of planting, harvesting, and cooking with voiceover explanations, creating a visual flowchart that traces the transformation of products and highlights the importance of each stage.

Learners explore digital books about food journeys and changes in Epic, selecting a story to read and summarise. They share interesting facts about where food comes from and how it is transformed for different purposes.

Students make visual flowcharts showing the stages of a food product's journey (e.g., milk to cheese), using the iPad camera to capture real-life examples and arranging them in PicCollage. They can add stickers and simple labels to highlight key changes.

Students use Sphero Indi to simulate the journey of food from farm to table, programming the car to move through stages like planting, harvesting, processing, and eating. As they navigate each step, learners discuss how products change and the importance of each process, reinforcing systems thinking through play.

Students code simple animations showing the transformation of a food product (e.g., a seed growing into a plant, harvested, and turned into bread). This introduces basic sequencing and computational thinking while reinforcing the unit's central idea.

Students work in groups to design and build a hands-on conveyor system using recycled cardboard, popsticks, tubes, and simple craft materials to represent the journey of food from farm to table. They create physical models of food items (like clay fruits, vegetables, or bread), then use Bee-Bots programmed with simple routes to 'transport' these items along their custom-built conveyor paths through different stations: farm, processing, market, home. Each station is decorated and labeled by students, who collaboratively decide how food changes at each step. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate food journeys, plan their conveyor layouts, build and decorate stations, test Bee-Bot routes, and reflect on how food transforms before reaching their plates.

plannerText: Prep students investigate where food comes from, how it changes for different purposes, and develop an appreciation for food. Activities include planting seeds, tracing food origins, creating flowcharts, visiting a farm, and exploring food waste and composting, all to understand the process of change in the products people use.

ci: The properties of materials influence how objects move and their use.
lo: Properties of materials; How objects move; How we use materials

s.d: Students use Freeform on iPads to collaboratively sort and classify materials by their properties, drawing and labelling digital diagrams. They experiment with moving digital objects to predict and test how different materials might roll, slide, or bounce, then capture their findings in a shared visual workspace.

Students use the Geoboard app to model different materials and shapes, experimenting with how changing properties affect movement and use. They create digital representations of objects and test how their designs might move or function in real life.

Set up a hands-on investigation where students use Sphero Indi cars and different surface materials (e.g., felt, sandpaper, cardboard, foil) to explore how material properties affect movement. Students predict, test, and record which surfaces make Indi go faster, slower, or stop, then share their findings with the class.

Students go on a 'material hunt' around the classroom or playground, taking photos of objects made from various materials. They use PicCollage to sort and label their findings by material type and movement (e.g., things that roll, slide, bounce), creating vibrant visual posters to display and discuss.

Prep students use ScratchJr to create simple animations showing how different objects move (e.g., rolling, sliding, bouncing) based on their material. They select backgrounds and characters, then program short sequences to visually represent their understanding of movement and material properties.

Students work in teams to design and build a large, interactive marble run using a variety of physical materials (cardboard tubes, recycled boxes, fabric, plastic containers, popsticks, clay, etc.) to explore how different surfaces and materials affect the speed, direction, and movement of marbles. Using iPads, students record short prediction videos, time their marble's journey with a simple timer app, and capture slow-motion clips to observe movement. They iterate their designs based on what they see, experimenting with adding ramps, tunnels, and barriers made from different materials to test friction and movement. The project follows the Design Cycle: students imagine, plan, create, test, and improve their marble runs, sharing their findings and videos in a class showcase.

plannerText: Prep students explore how the properties of materials influence movement and use through hands-on investigations, sensory experiences, and creative design challenges. Activities include experimenting with different materials, predicting and testing movement, sorting and classifying objects, and designing solutions to real-world problems using accessible, age-appropriate tools.

ci: An inquiry into rights and responsibilities in the struggle to share finite resources with other people and with other living things.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Family histories shape who we are and what we value
lo: What makes a family; The people in your family; Your family history; What is important to your family

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to create family history timelines, programming the car to travel along a path marked with key family events and traditions. As Indi stops at each point, students share stories and artefacts, connecting personal history to the central idea in a dynamic, interactive way.

Students take photos of family artefacts or family tree drawings with the iPad camera, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate these objects or family members, recording short stories or facts about their family history and values.

Students record short podcasts sharing a favourite family tradition or value, explaining how it connects to their family history and identity. They use Adobe Express Podcasting to add simple sound effects and music, creating engaging audio stories that bring their personal histories to life and connect to the unit's central idea.

Students design a vibrant family collage using PicCollage, layering family photos, cultural symbols, and images of special celebrations. They can add stickers and drawings to highlight what is important to their family.
Students use ScratchJr to create simple animated stories about a family tradition or journey, sequencing events and adding voiceovers to explain how their family's history shapes who they are.

Students design and build their own 'time capsules' using recycled boxes, clay, fabric, and craft materials to represent their family's unique history and culture. Each capsule includes handmade artifacts: mini family trees, clay figures, drawn maps of family origins, and crafted symbols of cultural celebrations. Students use Bee-Bots to deliver their time capsules along a collaboratively built timeline mat, programmed to stop at key 'moments' (e.g., family migration, special traditions). This project integrates physical making, storytelling, and early coding, allowing students to share their personal histories in a creative, hands-on, and interactive way.

plannerText: Prep students explore how family histories shape identity and values through activities like creating family trees, mapping family origins, sharing cultural celebrations, and connecting personal stories to broader concepts of place and time. The unit emphasizes visual, hands-on, and collaborative learning experiences, with strong links to art, music, and literacy.

ci: Schools are organised to help students learn and work together
lo: The organisation of the Junior School; Routines that allow developing independence; How to make the classroom and playground healthy and safe

s.d: Learners use Sphero Indi to explore and map out the different spaces and routines within the school. By programming Indi to visit key locations and narrating what happens in each, students build independence and understanding of how schools are organised to help everyone learn and work together.

Students use Freeform to collaboratively design a digital map of their classroom and playground, adding icons for routines and safety features. This interactive board helps them visualise how their school is organised to support learning and wellbeing.

Students record short audio clips sharing their favourite school spaces or daily routines, using Adobe Express Podcasting to create a collaborative class podcast that celebrates how the Junior School is organised to help them learn and work together. This activity supports oral language development and allows Prep learners to express their understanding of school roles, routines, and safety in a fun, accessible format.

Students take photos of classroom helpers, teachers, or important school objects (like the library or playground) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with their own voices, explaining their roles or how to use the space safely. This makes learning about school organisation fun and memorable.

Use PicCollage on iPads for students to visually map out the people who help them at school. They can combine photos, stickers, and drawings to create a vibrant collage showing the network of support in the Junior School, reinforcing the central idea of working together.

Students collaboratively design and build a large 3D model of their classroom and playground using cardboard, recycled materials, popsticks, and clay. After constructing key areas (desks, cubbies, play zones, etc.), they create and decorate miniature figures representing themselves and staff. Using age-appropriate tech like Bee-Bots, students program simple routes for their figures to follow daily routines (e.g., handwashing, snack time, lining up, safe play), integrating physical pathways and obstacles they build. As a final step, students add interactive elements (e.g., movable signs, opening doors, or healthy habit posters) that reinforce safety and independence. The process follows the Design Cycle: students imagine, plan, build, test Bee-Bot journeys, and reflect on how their design supports a healthy, safe, and independent school environment.

plannerText: This unit introduces Prep students to the organisation of Wesley College Junior School, focusing on routines that develop independence and ways to keep the classroom and playground healthy and safe. Students explore school spaces, roles, and responsibilities through tours, discussions, and hands-on activities, using visual and oral reflections to demonstrate their understanding.

ci: People use imagination and materials to express stories, ideas and culture through performance.
lo: Different materials to make puppets; Types and uses of puppets by different cultures; Features and purposes of puppet performances

s.d: Students design their own imaginative puppet characters and backgrounds in Delightex, then bring them to life by importing these visuals into Puppet Pals on iPads. They can record original puppet performances, combining their artwork, voice, and movement to express stories and cultural traditions in a highly creative, interactive way.

Students take photos of their handmade puppets or cultural puppet images, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the puppets' mouths and record their voices telling a story or sharing a cultural fact. This makes storytelling accessible, fun, and highly engaging for Year 1 learners.

Students record short audio stories using Adobe Express Podcasting, sharing their puppet characters' adventures and cultural backgrounds through voice and sound effects. This activity deepens their understanding of storytelling and performance by focusing on expressive oral narration and creative use of audio to bring their puppet stories to life.

Students collaboratively create a simple survey using Microsoft Forms to gather classmates' favourite types of puppets and puppet performances. They then analyse the collected data to identify popular features and purposes of puppetry across cultures, producing a visual summary of their findings to share with the class.

Students create vibrant digital collages showcasing different types of puppets from around the world, using photos of their own handmade puppets and images sourced from class materials. They arrange and label puppet features and cultural elements, then add simple captions to explain the stories and ideas each puppet expresses, bringing their learning to life in a colourful visual story.

Students will design and build their own shadow puppets inspired by different world cultures using materials like cardboard, skewers, colored cellophane, and recycled items. Working in teams, they will construct a collaborative shadow theater stage with a white screen and LED lights. Using simple stop-motion apps on iPads, students will animate their puppet stories, blending live shadow performance with digital capture. The project follows the design cycle: students empathize by exploring cultural puppet traditions, define their story, ideate puppet designs, prototype and build their puppets and stage, test by performing and recording, and reflect on their creative process. This hands-on hybrid project celebrates storytelling, craftsmanship, and digital creativity.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in the world of puppetry, exploring how imagination and materials are used to express stories, ideas, and culture through performance. Students investigate different types of puppets, their cultural significance, and the features of puppet shows, culminating in the creation and performance of their own puppet stories using handmade puppets and digital tools.

ci: Communities work together by creating systems to help people live and learn.
lo: The roles people play in a community; Systems and routines that help us stay organised; How communities work together to meet needs

s.d: Students collaboratively design an interactive digital 'Community Systems Map' using Delightex, where they visually map out the different roles, routines, and helpers in their community.
