s.d: Students design and prototype interactive 3D models of disaster-resistant shelters or emergency tools using Tinkercad, then bring their creations to life with Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record AR video walkthroughs using the iPad camera, narrating their design thinking and how their solution meets a specific community need. This immersive, hands-on workflow makes abstract design concepts tangible and engaging.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?Revamp Melbourne? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/revamp-melbourne) to explore perspective, choices and how people respond to change. They capture 2?3 screenshots or signs from the world and turn them into a reflection slide or gallery note explaining the choice, challenge or perspective shown in the world.

Students become hazard heroes by designing and coding disaster response devices with Micro:bit, inspired by the 'Night Safety' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=night-safety). They create wearable alerts or safety systems, connecting their design thinking to real-world community needs and resilience. This project brings STEM and social responsibility together in a dynamic, student-led way.

Teams publish interactive digital journals in Book Creator, combining photos, videos, voice recordings, and diagrams to document each stage of the design thinking process. This supports reflection, self-assessment, and sharing with the wider school community.

Students collaborate to design, code, and pilot CoDrone EDU drones in simulated rescue missions, responding to natural hazards and community needs. By iterating on drone designs and flight plans, learners apply the design thinking process and problem-solving skills to real-world challenges. This hands-on robotics experience brings the unit's focus on purposeful design and innovation to life.

Students work in teams to identify a natural hazard affecting a community (e.g., flood, earthquake, bushfire) and design a hands-on rescue mission scenario. Using Sphero Indi robots as 'rescue vehicles,' students physically build a detailed hazard landscape with recycled materials?such as rivers, collapsed bridges, blocked roads, and shelters?on large cardboard bases. They craft movable barriers, construct safe zones, and create obstacles that mirror real-world challenges. Teams then program their Sphero Indi to autonomously navigate the custom-built terrain, deliver 'emergency supplies' (represented by small crafted tokens), and reach affected areas. Throughout, students document their design thinking process, test and iterate their physical and digital prototypes, and present their final rescue solutions to an audience, explaining how their design addresses both the hazard and community needs.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore how people design for purpose through the lens of natural hazards and community needs. They engage in the design thinking process, research real-world problems, collaborate in teams, prototype innovative solutions, and pitch their ideas to an authentic audience, integrating empathy, data analysis, and persuasive communication throughout.

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

s.d: Students design and build interactive 3D models of their 'ideal community' or economic system using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They then use the iPad camera to record a virtual guided tour, narrating how wealth, resources, and responsibilities are distributed in their imagined society. This merges tactile, spatial, and digital storytelling for a futuristic approach to economic systems.

Launch a collaborative 'Wealth is More Than Money' digital wall where students post multimedia reflections (audio, drawings, photos of their Pop Art, or short videos) exploring non-monetary forms of wealth in their lives and communities. Students then use Seesaw to create digital portfolios tracking their evolving understanding of economic systems and personal values.

Students produce an 'Economic Voices' podcast, simulating interviews with historical and contemporary figures about economic systems, wealth, and responsibilities. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), they script, record, and edit episodes that bring economic concepts to life through storytelling and debate.

Students collaboratively design and visually present their 'Ideal Community' economic systems using Canva. Each group creates an interactive infographic or digital poster that showcases how wealth is distributed beyond money, the variety of jobs and income sources, and the responsibilities of citizens in managing resources. Students include diagrams of resource flows, annotated maps of their community layout, and illustrated scenarios showing budgeting and decision-making. The final Canva presentations are shared with the class for peer feedback, simulating a real-world pitch to a city council.

Students use Excel to model personal or community budgets, experimenting with different income sources, expenses, and savings strategies. They create interactive spreadsheets that visually demonstrate the consequences of financial decisions, supporting critical thinking about economic responsibility and resource allocation.

s.t: Wealth Web: Community Resource Exchange Game
s.d: Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively invent and build a hands-on board game that models how communities organise, share, and grow different forms of wealth (not just money). In teams, they design a 3D 'community map' using cardboard, clay, and recycled materials to represent neighbourhoods, resources (like skills, time, food, knowledge), and opportunities. They then integrate programmable Sphero Indi robots as 'community couriers'?students code them to follow colour-coded tracks between locations, simulating the flow of resources and jobs. Game mechanics, designed and prototyped by students, include physical tokens, custom dice, and event cards, all crafted by hand. The final game is play-tested by peers, requiring players to cooperate, trade, and solve challenges to build a thriving, equitable community?making abstract economic concepts tangible and dynamic through creative making and digital-physical integration.

plannerText: Students inquire into economic systems by exploring wealth beyond money, responsibilities in managing income, and diverse ways income is earned and managed. They engage in provocations, debates, budgeting simulations, job applications, and collaborative projects to design ideal communities, culminating in creative presentations and hands-on design challenges that connect economic concepts to real-world contexts.

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of natural disasters (e.g., bushfires, earthquakes, floods) using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app, then record AR video explanations using the iPad camera. They can app smash with Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to place themselves inside their AR scenes, narrating the science and human impact for a truly immersive digital artifact.

Students work in teams to design interactive, visually engaging digital posters or infographics that map the risks and impacts of bushfires, earthquakes, and floods across Australia. Using Canva's mapping, chart, and infographic features, students plot real disaster data, highlight at-risk communities, and annotate with their own research findings. They then prototype and pitch innovative solutions for disaster resilience, embedding diagrams, timelines, and children's rights perspectives. The final Canva presentations are shared in a class expo, allowing for peer feedback and reflection.

Students engineer early warning wearables for natural disasters using Micro:bit, guided by the 'Night Safety' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=night-safety). They code devices that simulate alerts for bushfires, floods, or earthquakes, linking science, technology, and community safety. This hands-on project empowers students to turn empathy into action through innovation.

Students collaborate to research natural disasters like bushfires, floods, and earthquakes, then script and record a podcast episode explaining how structural features and adaptations help living things survive these events. Using Adobe Express Podcasting, they produce a clear audio presentation that includes scientific explanations and community safety advice, demonstrating their understanding of the interactions between living things, materials, and light. This podcast can be shared with peers to raise awareness and support informed decision-making about disaster preparedness.

Students code CoDrone EDU drones to simulate early warning systems for natural disasters, such as bushfires or floods. By programming drones to detect and respond to environmental cues, learners explore the intersection of technology, science, and community safety. This real-world challenge empowers students to innovate for disaster resilience.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and prototype wearable early warning devices that help communities respond to bushfires, earthquakes, or floods. First, they research real-world warning systems and brainstorm features that could alert and protect children during disasters. In teams, students physically craft wearable prototypes (e.g., wristbands, badges, or headbands) using materials like felt, recycled plastics, Velcro, LEDs, conductive thread, and cardboard. They integrate a simple programmable sensor (such as a Makey Makey or Circuit Playground Express) to simulate detecting a hazard?triggering lights, sounds, or vibrations. Students code their device (e.g., using block-based coding) to respond to 'danger signals' (like shaking for earthquakes or a heat source for bushfires). The project culminates in a showcase where students pitch their wearable's design, demo its warning features, and reflect on how it could uphold children's rights and safety in disaster-prone areas.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 5 students in the science of natural disasters, focusing on bushfires, earthquakes, and floods. Through collaborative inquiry, students research hazards, analyse data, map risks, and use the design thinking process to prototype and pitch solutions that protect communities and uphold children's rights. The unit integrates maths, science, geography, and digital technologies, culminating in student-led presentations and reflections.

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of local flora and fauna using the Merge Cube and iPads, then use the Merge Explorer app to overlay digital information about conservation needs and stewardship actions. They can record video tours of their models using the iPad camera, narrating how their community can interact with and protect these species.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?Climate Futures The Farm? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/climate-futures-the-farm) to explore human impact, habitats and possible environmental action. They capture 2?3 screenshots or signs from the world and turn them into a short conservation brief showing the issue, the evidence and one realistic action.

After a nature walk around the school, students use Book Creator to compile a multimedia field guide of native plants and animals, including photos, audio reflections, and conservation tips tailored to their local environment.

Students will design and code interactive micro:bit devices to raise awareness about local endangered species and promote conservation actions within the school community. Working in teams, students will research a native animal or plant under threat, then program their micro:bit to display scrolling messages, icons, or alerts highlighting key facts and urgent actions (e.g., 'Save the Growling Grass Frog! Plant native reeds!'). Students will install their micro:bits around the school as digital advocacy signs, collect peer feedback, and reflect on how technology can empower communities to become environmental stewards. This hands-on project links digital creativity with real-world sustainability and local stewardship.

Learners design and code CoDrone EDU drones to monitor and map local waterways, simulating how technology supports environmental conservation. Teams collect data, navigate obstacles, and propose solutions for protecting river ecosystems, directly linking to the unit's focus on community-environment interactions. This hands-on robotics project inspires stewardship and scientific inquiry.

Students work in teams to design and construct a large, modular river ecosystem model using recycled cardboard, clay, fabric, and natural materials. They research local waterways and First Nations perspectives to inform the ecosystem's features?such as wetlands, native plant zones, and animal habitats. Integrating Sphero Indi robots, students program the robots to represent 'water flow' or animal migration, navigating through their hand-built landscape. As part of the Design Cycle, students iteratively test and refine their physical models to ensure the 'water' can move through the system without blockages, simulating the impact of pollution, conservation efforts, and community interventions. The final ecosystem includes interactive elements (e.g., removable pollution sources, movable animals, or plant growth markers) and is presented to the school community with student-led explanations of conservation choices and stewardship strategies.

plannerText: Students inquire into how communities interact with the environment, focusing on conservation, stewardship, and the influence of community values on environmental management. Key activities include exploring First Nations perspectives, researching local flora and fauna, investigating council initiatives, and pitching persuasive sustainability actions for the school community.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students create interactive 3D artefacts representing key resources discovered in Australia (e.g., gold, wheat, wool) using Merge Cube. They then app smash by embedding their 3D models and explanatory videos into a digital Book Creator journal, allowing for immersive storytelling about how each resource shaped the nation's growth.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?Revamp Melbourne? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/revamp-melbourne) to explore human impact, habitats and possible environmental action. They capture 2?3 screenshots or signs from the world and turn them into a short conservation brief showing the issue, the evidence and one realistic action.

Learners create a Gold Rush podcast series, narrating stories from the perspectives of migrants, Indigenous Australians, and colonial settlers. Using Adobe Express Podcasting (https://podcast.adobe.com), students blend research, interviews, and dramatic storytelling to explore how resource discovery shaped nations and changed lives.

Students will work in teams to create an interactive, visually rich timeline infographic in Canva that traces the impact of Australia's Gold Rush on migration, Indigenous communities, and colonial society. Each group will research a key event or perspective (e.g., an Indigenous voice, a migrant's journey, environmental change) and design a timeline segment with images, maps, and primary source quotes. The final class timeline will be published as a collaborative digital display, allowing students to explore cause and effect, compare perspectives, and visually connect economic, social, and environmental changes over time.

Students program CoDrone EDU drones to simulate gold rush prospecting missions, navigating obstacle courses that represent historical challenges. Teams iterate on drone designs to transport 'resources' efficiently, connecting technological innovation to the growth and development of nations. This engaging activity brings history and STEM together in an unforgettable way.

s.t: Gold Rush Innovation Trail: Interactive Prospecting Adventure
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large, physical 'prospecting trail' inspired by Australia's goldfields, using recycled materials, cardboard, clay, and fabric to create realistic terrain, dig sites, rivers, and historical landmarks. Integrating Ozobot robots, students program them to represent gold-seekers navigating the trail, encountering challenges such as Indigenous land boundaries, environmental obstacles, and encounters with other migrants. Each stop features student-made, interactive artifacts (e.g., pop-up tents, sluice boxes, mine entrances) that trigger physical mechanisms (levers, pulleys) or QR codes for digital mini-narratives. The project follows the Design Cycle: students research gold rush history, ideate trail features and challenges, prototype structures and mechanisms, test Ozobot navigation, and refine both physical and digital elements for an immersive, hands-on exploration of how resource discovery shaped migration, innovation, and impact on communities.

plannerText: This unit explores how the discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations, focusing on Australia's Gold Rush, migration, and the effects on Indigenous and colonial populations. Students investigate historical events, analyze cause and effect, research case studies, and create timelines, infographics, and perspective-based narratives to deepen their understanding of economic, social, and environmental change.

ci: Managing change requires acceptance, knowledge and strategies.
lo: Establishing, managing and maintaining relationships; Managing behaviours in digital situations; Managing changes associated with puberty

s.t: Merge Cubes + iPads (Augmented Reality Self-Portrait Gallery)
s.d: Students create digital self-portraits using iPads, then use Merge Cubes to transform their artwork into interactive 3D AR experiences. Each cube face can represent a different aspect of their identity or a strategy for managing change, allowing peers to explore and 'unlock' stories about acceptance, growth, and resilience in an immersive gallery walk.

s.t: Seesaw App Smash (Digital Wellbeing Journals + ChatterPix Kids)
s.d: Students maintain a digital wellbeing journal in Seesaw, reflecting on strategies for managing change and emotional responses. For key moments, they use ChatterPix Kids to animate a self-portrait or symbol, recording their voice to share personal insights or advice about navigating change, then embed these videos in their Seesaw journal.

Students collaboratively design empathy map posters in Canva, visually representing the feelings, needs, and strategies of someone experiencing change (e.g., starting at a new school, going through puberty, or facing online challenges). These can be shared digitally or printed for classroom discussion.

Students create and publish digital sticky notes on a shared Padlet board, each detailing a strategy, resource, or personal story related to managing change in relationships, digital behaviours, or puberty. They actively comment on and 'like' peers? posts, collaboratively building a dynamic resource bank that reflects their growing knowledge and acceptance of change. This interactive wall serves as a visual and evolving product that connects directly to the unit?s central idea of managing change through acceptance, knowledge, and strategies.

Each student authors a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, combining text, images, audio reflections, and video clips to document their learning about managing change, acceptance, and growth. The final product serves as a personal guide and can be shared with younger students or families.

s.t: Transitions Time Capsule: Interactive Memory Vaults
s.d: Students use the Design Cycle to collaboratively design and build physical 'time capsules' from recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, each representing a key transition or change in their lives (e.g., moving schools, making new friends, learning new skills). Each capsule features a secret compartment and a digital element: students embed programmable micro:bit devices (or similar) to add interactive features such as sound messages, LED patterns, or simple digital counters that activate when the capsule is opened. Students prototype, test, and iterate both the mechanical and digital components, reflecting on the emotions and strategies associated with change. The final interactive vaults are shared in a class 'Transitions Gallery,' where peers unlock, explore, and discuss the stories and wellbeing strategies hidden inside each capsule.

plannerText: This unit explores how managing change requires acceptance, knowledge, and strategies, with a focus on relationships, digital behaviours, and personal growth. Students engage in reflective journaling, digital art, empathy mapping, and collaborative discussions, culminating in creative projects that support wellbeing and positive transitions.

ci: The media engages its audience and invites a response
lo: How media targets audiences; Techniques used to engage and persuade; Critical evaluation of media messages

s.d: Students design and build immersive 3D virtual 'advertising worlds' where visitors interact with persuasive media, ethical dilemmas, and alternative consumer models. Each group creates a virtual experience that challenges peers to think critically about advertising influence and sustainability, directly linking to the unit's focus on media engagement and audience response.

Students collaborate to script and record persuasive podcasts that analyse how media targets audiences and uses techniques to engage and persuade. They incorporate interviews, sound effects, and music to critically evaluate media messages and present their findings as compelling audio stories on ethical consumer choices. This hands-on activity deepens understanding of media influence and invites authentic audience response.

Students compose and produce original persuasive jingles, slam poems, or rap songs that critique or promote ethical consumer choices. These audio creations are shared as part of their multimodal presentations, directly supporting the unit's emphasis on media, persuasion, and creative response.

Students create dynamic, data-driven infographics and digital posters that visually communicate the impact of advertising strategies, peer influence, and environmental consequences of consumer choices. Canva's 'Remove Background' feature can be used to remix product images, replacing the banned green screen kits.

Students work in collaborative teams to create a persuasive multimedia advertising campaign using Adobe Express. Each group selects a real-world product and researches its ethical and environmental impacts, as well as how media and advertising shape consumer choices. Students script, record, and edit their own video advertisements, integrating original slogans, images, and voiceovers. They use Adobe Express's video and design tools to combine survey/interview data, peer influence analysis, and researched facts into a compelling, ethical ad. Finished campaigns are published and shared with the class for peer feedback, sparking discussion about responsible consumerism and the power of media influence.

s.t: Persuasion Arcade: Build & Code an Interactive Ethical Shopping Game
s.d: Students work in teams to design and construct a physical 'arcade booth' using recycled cardboard, LED lights, and simple circuits. Each booth represents a different consumer scenario (e.g., buying sneakers, snacks, or tech) and features physical product models crafted from clay or recycled materials. Using Micro:bit or Makey Makey, students program interactive buttons or touchpads that trigger digital media (such as audio jingles, persuasive slogans, or ethical dilemma videos) displayed on an iPad or laptop embedded in the booth. Players navigate choices by pressing buttons, with outcomes and feedback reflecting the influence of advertising, peer pressure, and ethical considerations. The project follows the Design Cycle: students empathize by researching real consumer dilemmas, ideate creative booth concepts, prototype with both physical and digital elements, test with peers, and reflect on how their arcade influences thinking about ethical consumerism.

plannerText: Students collaboratively investigate how media and advertising influence consumer choices, conduct surveys and interviews to analyse peer and social influences, and research the ethical and environmental impacts of products. They present findings through multimodal texts (including songs, advertisements, and slam poems) and take action by creating persuasive media or advocating for more ethical consumer practices.

ci: An inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities; the structure and function of organisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Students create 3D models of interconnected human-made systems?like supply chains or transportation networks?on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate the flow of goods, information, and influence. This app smash brings abstract systems thinking to life, allowing learners to manipulate variables and observe ripple effects in real time. The activity supports inquiry into how advertising, peer influence, and ethical choices shape communities and consumer behaviour.

Groups create and distribute digital surveys to the school community to gather authentic data on peer influences, advertising effectiveness, and ethical considerations in consumer choices. Students then analyse the results in Excel to identify trends and present their findings as part of their inquiry into how organisations shape consumer behaviour.

Students design and code ethical pop-up market systems using Micro:bit, inspired by the 'Data Handling' lesson (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary). They gather and analyse data on consumer choices, simulate transactions, and reflect on the impact of their decisions. This project fuses economics, ethics, and digital innovation.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?Alternative Energy? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/alternative-energy) to explore human impact, habitats and possible environmental action. They capture 2?3 screenshots or signs from the world and turn them into a short conservation brief showing the issue, the evidence and one realistic action.

Students design and code CoDrone EDU drones to model ethical supply chains, simulating the journey of goods from production to consumer. By programming drones to make decisions at each stage, learners explore the interconnectedness of systems and the impact of ethical choices. This interactive experience deepens understanding of responsible action in global markets.

s.t: Ethical Pop-Up Market: Build & Code Smart Stalls
s.d: Students work in teams to design, build, and code a physical 'pop-up market' stall that showcases a product with ethical and sustainable features. Using recycled materials, cardboard, and craft supplies, each group constructs a stall with interactive elements (e.g., spinning wheels, product displays, moving signs). They embed simple programmable electronics (such as micro:bit or Makey Makey) to add digital features like sound effects, scrolling LED messages, or touch-activated info panels that communicate the ethical story behind their product. The design cycle guides students through empathising with ethical consumers, ideating fair trade or eco-friendly product concepts, prototyping their stall, testing interactive features, and refining their presentation. The final market is set up in the Makerspace, where peers and visitors can 'shop', interact with the stalls, and reflect on how advertising, peer influence, and ethical considerations shape consumer choices.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 6 students in an inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities, focusing on how advertising, peer influence, and ethical considerations shape consumer choices. Students collaboratively research, analyse, and present findings on selected products, create persuasive multimodal texts, and explore alternative models of consumerism that honour fairness and sustainability.

ci: Scientists undertake experimentation to confirm, contest and create new theories.
lo: The scientific method; How experiments lead to new understandings; The relationship between planets and the Sun; Energy transfer and transformation in circuits

s.d: Students use Micro:bit to collect and analyse data from scientific experiments, bringing the scientific method to life as they investigate planetary motion or energy transfer. They visualise their findings and draw conclusions, deepening their understanding of how experimentation leads to new theories. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary)

Students create augmented reality models of electrical circuits, reversible/irreversible changes, or planetary systems using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app. They can record short video explanations overlaying their AR models, demonstrating their understanding of scientific concepts and inquiry methods.

Students use CoDrone EDU drones to conduct scientific experiments, such as measuring distances, simulating planetary motion, or testing variables in controlled trials. By collecting and analysing data from drone flights, learners experience the scientific method in action. This hands-on approach fosters curiosity and critical thinking in physical science.

Students design and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect data on classmates' hypotheses, experiment results, or scientific misconceptions. They analyze the data in Excel, drawing evidence-based conclusions and reflecting on the importance of data in scientific inquiry.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?Alternative Energy? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/alternative-energy) to explore perspective, choices and how people respond to change. They capture 2?3 screenshots or signs from the world and turn them into a reflection slide or gallery note explaining the choice, challenge or perspective shown in the world.

s.t: Planetary Motion Marble Run: Code & Construct
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large-scale, multi-level marble run that models planetary movement and energy transfer. Using recycled materials (cardboard, tubes, tape, popsticks), they physically construct tracks that represent orbits, inclines for gravitational slingshots, and obstacles for celestial events. Teams integrate micro:bit sensors (or similar age-appropriate microcontrollers) to digitally track marbles (planets) as they travel, displaying data (speed, distance, 'orbital period') on a connected tablet. Students program the micro:bit to trigger lights or sounds at key events (e.g., 'eclipse' zones, energy transfers). The project culminates in a science fair demonstration where students explain how their hybrid creation models scientific concepts of motion, energy, and planetary systems, and reflect on how scientists use both physical models and digital tools to test and refine theories.

plannerText: This unit immerses students in scientific inquiry through hands-on experiments in physical science, chemistry, and earth/space science. Students follow the scientific method to investigate energy transfer, chemical changes, and planetary movement, culminating in a science fair where they present their own experiments and reflect on how scientists confirm, contest, and create new theories.

ci: The influences on our consumer choices reveal the interdependence between human and natural systems and call for responsible action toward just and sustainable futures.
lo: Influences on consumer choices (function); How consumer decisions impact living things and their habitats (causation); Alternative models of consumerism that honour a fair and sustainable future (perspective)

s.d: Students collaboratively design immersive 3D virtual worlds in Delightex to visualize the entire lifecycle of a consumer product, from resource extraction to disposal. They then embed Padlet boards within their worlds for peers to leave critical feedback, questions, and alternative sustainable ideas, creating a dynamic, interactive exhibition that brings the complexities of consumer choices and sustainability to life.

Students create and distribute digital surveys using Microsoft Forms to gather real data from their peers and families about consumer habits, influences, and attitudes toward sustainability. They analyze the results to identify trends and present their findings as evidence in their group investigations.

Students use Micro:bit to collect and visualise data about their own consumer choices, exploring the interdependence between humans and natural systems. They analyse patterns and propose actions for more sustainable living, making data-driven decisions for real-world impact. (https://microbit.org/teach/lessons/?selected=data-handling-unit-summary)

Students compose and record original slam poems, raps, or jingles in GarageBand, using literary devices and sound design to creatively persuade listeners to reflect on their consumer habits and consider alternative, sustainable models.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?Climate Futures Transportation? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/climate-futures-transportation) to explore human impact, habitats and possible environmental action. They capture 2?3 screenshots or signs from the world and turn them into a short conservation brief showing the issue, the evidence and one realistic action.

s.t: Sustainable Product Pitch: Smart Packaging Prototypes
s.d: Students work in teams to design, build, and prototype an innovative, eco-friendly product package that addresses an environmental issue caused by consumer choices (e.g., single-use plastics, food waste, over-packaging). Using recycled materials (cardboard, bottles, fabric, etc.), they construct a physical prototype of their packaging solution, integrating a micro:bit or Makey Makey to add an interactive digital feature (such as a touch sensor that plays a persuasive audio message, or a display showing the environmental impact of their design). Students use the Design Cycle: researching existing packaging, brainstorming and sketching solutions, building prototypes, testing with peers, and refining based on feedback. Each team creates a short video pitch using iPads, showcasing their prototype's features and sustainability benefits to a mock 'eco-investor' panel.

plannerText: Year 6 students investigate how influences shape consumer choices and the resulting impact on the world's sustainable future. They analyze advertising, peer influence, and environmental consequences through research, surveys, and case studies, culminating in collaborative presentations and creative action projects that advocate for just and sustainable consumer models.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human needs drive the development of nations.
lo: How human needs drive national development; Role of migration in nation building; Impacts of development on Indigenous and colonial populations

s.d: Students build 3D migration journeys or nation-building timelines on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate key events, push-pull factors, and personal stories. This app smash helps learners visualise the forces that drive the development of nations and the lived experiences of migration, deepening empathy and historical understanding. The interactive models serve as powerful tools for sharing research and sparking discussion.

Students use the verified Minecraft Education lesson ?The Hall of Inventions? (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/challenges/the-hall-of-inventions) to explore how human needs have inspired inventions that shaped nations. They capture 2?3 screenshots of inventions or signs in the world and create a reflection slide explaining how these inventions address specific human needs and influence national development.

Facilitate a collaborative Padlet where students post and respond to migration stories, personal family histories, and reflections on citizenship. They can link to research, videos, and maps, fostering a living digital archive that connects classroom learning to real-world experiences.

Students author a multimedia eBook in Book Creator, weaving together first-person migration narratives, maps, and primary source analysis. They can include voice recordings, scanned artefacts, and interactive quizzes about the influence of systems on citizens.

Design and distribute a Microsoft Forms survey to the school community, gathering data on migration backgrounds, reasons for moving, and perceptions of Australian identity. Students analyse the results in Excel, then present their findings on how needs and systems influence migration and citizenship.

Students will research the journeys and experiences of diverse migrants to Australia, both pre- and post-colonisation. Using the Design Cycle, they will select a real or imagined migration story, then design and build a tangible, interactive 'memory machine'?a physical artifact that tells the story through movement, sound, and light. Students will craft the base using recycled materials, cardboard, and clay, constructing elements like turning wheels, pop-up scenes, or sliding panels. They will integrate simple programmable electronics (e.g., BBC micro:bit or Makey Makey) to trigger recorded oral histories, ambient sounds, or LED lights when parts are moved or touched. Each machine must physically embody key moments or challenges from the migration narrative, allowing viewers to 'travel' through the story by interacting with the device. This hands-on project encourages empathy, creativity, and a deep understanding of how migration shapes identity and nationhood.

plannerText: This unit explores how human needs drive the development of nations by comparing pre- and post-colonised Australia, examining the relationship between migration and needs, and analysing the influence of systems on citizens. Students engage in historical inquiry, personal narrative, museum excursions, and creative projects to understand migration, citizenship, and the shaping of Australian identity.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Learners create interactive 3D brain models or wellbeing maps on Merge Cube, then use Delightex to animate neural pathways, zones of regulation, or growth mindset strategies. This app smash makes neurodiversity and self-regulation visible, empowering students to explore how their brains work and how experiences shape learning and wellbeing. The activity supports reflection and collaborative discussion about personal growth and health.

Students document their personal wellbeing strategies by creating a daily 'Wellbeing Vlog' using the iPad Camera, then upload and reflect on their progress in Seesaw. This app-smashing workflow encourages self-management, goal setting, and peer feedback on strategies for health, safety, and emotional regulation.

Students program their Micro:bit to build a step counter that tracks their daily physical activity by coding the accelerometer sensor to detect movement. They test and refine their code to ensure accurate step counts, then analyse their data to reflect on how physical activity supports their wellbeing, linking to the central idea of personal health and self-awareness.

Students create a digital 'Neurodiversity Storybook' in Book Creator, illustrating how different brains learn and thrive. Each page can feature a unique learning profile, myth-busting facts, and student-generated wellbeing tips, integrating research, creativity, and empathy.

Students will collaboratively design an interactive infographic in Canva titled 'My Brain, My Mindset'. Each student researches and illustrates a different aspect of neurodiversity, brain physiology, or growth mindset (e.g., parts of the brain, how emotions affect learning, strategies for self-regulation). They use Canva's drag-and-drop features to combine diagrams, icons, and their own reflections, creating a visually engaging resource that explains how our brains and experiences shape who we are. The final product can be shared digitally or printed for a classroom display, sparking further discussion about identity, wellbeing, and personal growth.

s.t: Neurodiversity Quest: Build & Code Your Brain Adventure
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a large, interactive 3D 'brain map' maze using cardboard, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Each section of the maze physically represents a different brain region or function (e.g., memory, emotion, creativity, self-regulation), decorated with symbols and textures. Students embed simple electronics (e.g., Makey Makey, LED circuits) to trigger sounds, lights, or digital messages when touched. Using Sphero Indi robots or similar, students code a 'neurodiverse explorer' to navigate the maze, encountering hands-on challenges and reflection prompts at each station (e.g., a puzzle about growth mindset, a sensory task for emotional regulation). The project culminates in a showcase where teams guide others through their custom brain adventure, explaining the links between physical structures, mental health, and personal identity.

plannerText: This unit explores identities as individuals and collectives through physical, emotional, social, and spiritual health and wellbeing, with a focus on the physiology and function of the brain, the impact of mindset and experiences on learning, and strategies to support health, safety, and wellbeing. Students engage in reflective, creative, and collaborative activities to deepen their understanding of neurodiversity, self-regulation, and personal growth.

ci: People recognise important events through celebrations and traditions.
lo: The traditions of our families and communities; How and why people celebrate; The similarities and differences between celebrations

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to create a 'celebration parade,' programming the car to travel through stations representing different cultural events and traditions. As Indi visits each stop, students share stories, music, or artefacts, making global celebrations interactive and memorable.

Students photograph symbols or artefacts from celebrations important to their families or communities, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate these images and record a short explanation about the tradition or event. This activity helps students express their understanding of how people recognise important events through celebrations, connecting directly to the unit?s central idea and lines of inquiry about family and community traditions. The final product is a lively, recorded animation that showcases students? oral language skills and their reflections on similarities and differences between celebrations.

Students create and perform simple puppet shows using Puppet Pals to share stories about their favourite family or cultural celebrations. They select characters and backgrounds to represent key traditions, then record their voices narrating the significance of each celebration, bringing their personal and community traditions to life through digital storytelling.

Students create a digital celebration journal in Seesaw by drawing pictures, recording their voices sharing family traditions, and uploading photos of celebrations they experience. They explore the central idea by comparing their own celebrations with those of classmates, building a vibrant, multimodal collection that can be shared with families to showcase diverse traditions and important events.

Students create vibrant digital collages using photos, drawings, and text to showcase their family and community celebrations. They combine images of traditional foods, costumes, and decorations with short captions explaining the significance of each element, expressing their understanding of how and why people celebrate important events.

Students begin by talking with family or classmates to learn about special symbols used in their celebrations (Empathise). They define the key features that make these symbols meaningful (Define). Next, they brainstorm and sketch their own unique celebration symbol that represents an important event or tradition (Ideate). Using craft materials like paper, clay, fabric, and recycled items, they build a tangible model of their symbol (Prototype). Finally, students share their models with peers, explaining the meaning and receiving feedback to improve their design (Test).

plannerText: This Prep HWEO unit explores how people recognise important events through celebrations and traditions, with students engaging in cultural dances, storytelling, art (such as circle art), music, and hands-on activities like making traditional foods. The unit emphasises sharing personal and family traditions, comparing global celebrations, and expressing understanding through creative, multimodal experiences.

ci: We all have roles and responsibilities in the community
lo: Roles people play; Responsibilities in the community; How working together helps everyone

s.d: Learners use Sphero Indi to design a community map, programming the car to visit different helpers and locations within the school. As Indi travels, students explain each role and responsibility, reinforcing their understanding of how everyone contributes to a caring community.

Students take photos of themselves or classroom helpers, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short messages about their responsibilities, creating a playful digital 'talking roles' gallery.

Students create individual digital journal entries in Seesaw where they draw or photograph their classroom jobs or community helpers, then add simple voice recordings to explain their roles and responsibilities. The teacher compiles these entries into a shared class portfolio, allowing students to explore each other's contributions and reflect on how working together supports the community.

Learners film and edit short skits in iMovie, acting out different community roles and responsibilities. They collaborate to script, perform, and record scenes that show how helpers contribute to the school and neighbourhood, then share their movies with peers to spark discussion about teamwork and responsibility.

Use PicCollage on iPads for students to visually sort and group images of different community roles (e.g., teacher, nurse, firefighter, gardener), then present their collages to the class, explaining the importance of each role using simple voice recordings.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and build wearable accessories (hats, badges, belts, or vests) that represent different roles and responsibilities within the school community. Using recycled cardboard, felt, fabric scraps, and craft materials, each child designs and constructs a unique wearable for a community helper (e.g., teacher, gardener, crossing guard). Next, they embed simple tech by attaching a programmable LED badge (e.g., Glow and Go LED badge or similar) or a sound button to their wearable, which can light up or play a message when pressed. Students then role-play their community helper, explaining their responsibilities to the class while demonstrating how their wearable communicates their role. This project blends hands-on making, digital integration, and dramatic play to deepen understanding of community roles.

plannerText: In this unit, Prep students explore the central idea that everyone has roles and responsibilities in the community. Through stories, role play, visual timetables, and hands-on activities, they learn about different helpers in the school and wider community, develop essential agreements, and reflect on their own responsibilities as learners and friends.

ci: An inquiry into the natural world and its laws; the interaction between the natural world and human societies.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Human actions change the environment.
lo: How people interact with different environments; The effect people have on the environment; Ways in which people can take action and care for the environment

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to model the impact of human actions on different environments, programming the car to navigate through areas representing pollution, recycling, and conservation. As they guide Indi, students discuss how their choices can help or harm the planet, making environmental stewardship tangible and fun.

Students take photos of their ceramic or drawn marine animals and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, giving each creature a voice to explain how human actions affect their environment and what kids can do to help. This makes environmental advocacy personal and memorable.

Have students document their 'action for the environment' (e.g., picking up rubbish, using reusable containers) by taking photos or short videos in Seesaw. They can then record voice-overs explaining their choices, creating a digital portfolio of sustainable actions to share with families.

Students collaborate to create a class podcast series where each child records a short audio segment describing a local environment and a positive action they can take to care for it, reinforcing the central idea that human actions change the environment. Using Adobe Express Podcasting, learners practice speaking clearly and listen to each other?s contributions, then combine their recordings into a shared podcast that can be played for families and the school community to inspire environmental care.

Students create vibrant visual collages comparing healthy and unhealthy environments (e.g., clean reef vs. polluted reef) using PicCollage on iPads. They can use stickers, their own photos, and drawings to visually demonstrate cause and effect, then present their collages to the class.

Students work in teams to design and build detailed 3D habitats (desert, marine, playground) using recycled materials, clay, and craft supplies. They embed simple tech by adding programmable Bee-Bots or Blue-Bots into their dioramas, creating obstacle courses that represent real environmental challenges (like litter or barriers for animals). Students program the robots to 'travel' through the habitats, simulating the journey of an animal or a clean-up crew, and reflect on how their design choices impact the environment. The project follows the Design Cycle: students inquire about habitat needs and threats, plan their diorama and robot path, create and test their interactive model, then improve it based on observations and peer feedback.

plannerText: In this Prep 'Sharing the Planet' unit, students explore different environments (desert, marine, playground), investigate the impact of human actions such as pollution and recycling, and learn about responsibility and change. Activities include hands-on art, music inspired by the environment, excursions, and student-led action projects to care for the planet.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: An inquiry into orientation in place and time; personal histories; the relationship between and the interconnectedness of individuals and civilisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: To be healthy, people???s basic needs must be met.
lo: How our brain is growing and changing; How our emotions, behaviours and actions promote relationships; How we care for our bodies

s.d: Students use Sphero Indi to create a 'healthy habits' obstacle course, programming the car to travel through stations representing nutrition, exercise, and wellbeing. As Indi moves, students share tips and stories about meeting basic needs, making health education active and engaging.

Students draw or photograph their favourite healthy foods, exercise activities, or sleep routines, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with their own voices explaining why these choices help keep their bodies and minds healthy, directly connecting to the central idea of meeting basic needs.

Learners document their daily healthy habits by uploading photos or short videos of their lunchboxes, exercise, and calming techniques to Seesaw, adding voice reflections about how these habits help them feel energised and happy, supporting formative assessment and home-school connections.

Students create vibrant digital collages that showcase their understanding of healthy habits by combining photos, drawings, and text about nutrition, exercise, sleep, and wellbeing. They arrange images and words to tell their own 'Healthy Me' story, which can be shared with classmates and families to celebrate their learning about meeting basic needs for health.

Learners compose and record 'healthy habits' jingles in GarageBand, using rhythm and lyrics to reinforce messages about nutrition, exercise, and wellbeing. They share their catchy audio creations with the class, sparking conversations about how music can motivate positive lifestyle choices.

Students use the Design Cycle to design and build a 'home' or 'rest zone' for a small programmable Bee-Bot or Blue-Bot, representing a Wellbeing Buddy that needs healthy routines. Working in teams, students brainstorm what makes a space healthy and calming (e.g., nutritious food areas, quiet zones, exercise corners, sleep nooks). They physically construct these spaces using recycled boxes, fabric scraps, clay, popsticks, and craft materials, building furniture and decorations that represent healthy choices. Next, students plan and lay out paths for the bot to visit each space in a healthy daily routine sequence. Using age-appropriate coding on iPads, they program the bot to navigate their crafted home, stopping at each zone and explaining (with pre-recorded iPad audio or simple picture cards) how each area supports wellbeing. This project combines tangible building, digital programming, and creative storytelling to reinforce healthy habits in an immersive, hands-on way.

plannerText: This Prep unit explores the central idea that to be healthy, people's basic needs must be met, focusing on nutrition, exercise, sleep, wellbeing, and self-regulation. Students engage in activities such as classifying foods, learning about the 'Zones of Regulation', creating healthy lifestyle stories, and reflecting on their own habits through visual, hands-on, and collaborative experiences.

ci: People express their emotions through the way they communicate.
lo: Different ways to communicate emotions; How art, music and movement express feelings; Understanding others' expressions through body language and facial cues

s.d: Students create interactive digital storyboards in Delightex, then bring their own facial expressions and body language to life by recording themselves as characters in Puppet Pals. This workflow allows students to visually and physically express a range of emotions, then sequence and narrate their stories, directly connecting to the unit's focus on communicating feelings beyond words.

Students take photos of their own expressive faces or artwork, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record their voices describing different emotions. This empowers students to explore how tone, pitch, and expression combine to communicate feelings.

Students document and reflect on their emotional expressions through photos, drawings, and short audio clips, creating a digital portfolio of how they communicate feelings in different contexts (art, sport, drama, playground). Teachers can prompt students to respond to scenarios using expressive selfies or voice recordings.

Students create short, expressive video clips using iMovie where they act out different emotions and add simple titles or drawings to highlight how body language and facial expressions communicate feelings. This hands-on activity helps them explore the central idea by combining movement, visual cues, and storytelling in a fun, age-appropriate digital format.

Students create expressive digital collages using PicCollage to combine photos of their own facial expressions, hand-drawn artwork, and colorful backgrounds that represent different emotions. They arrange images and add simple text labels to communicate how feelings can be shown through art and body language, linking directly to the unit?s focus on multimodal emotional expression. This hands-on activity encourages exploration of color, pattern, and imagery to visually express emotions in a fun, age-appropriate way.

Students work in teams to design and build mini 'emotion playgrounds' that physically represent different feelings using a mix of craft materials (cardboard, fabric, recycled materials, string, pipe cleaners, etc.) and simple mechanisms (ramps, levers, pulleys). Each playground features several zones or stations, each inspired by an emotion (e.g., joy = bouncy bridge, sadness = gentle slide, excitement = spinning tunnel). Students use Sphero Indi robots with custom-decorated shells and coloured code tiles to navigate their playground, programming movement sequences that express and match each emotion's character (e.g., fast zig-zags for excitement, slow curves for calm). The design cycle guides students through empathising (discussing how emotions feel and look), ideating, prototyping playground zones, testing with their Indi, and refining both the physical build and robot path to best 'express' each feeling. The final showcase invites peers to interpret which emotions are being shown through both the playgrounds' design and the robots' expressive journeys.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring how people express emotions through communication, body language, facial expressions, art, music, and sport. Activities include studying artists like Frida Kahlo, creating expressive portraits, designing team identities, and using drama and movement to convey feelings, with a strong focus on multimodal and nonverbal communication.

ci: Systems are involved in preparing products for consumers.
lo: Where products come from; The systems products go through; How people choose their products

s.d: Students collaboratively design an interactive digital flowchart in Delightex to visually map the journey of a product (e.g., milk or apples) from farm to table, then use Seesaw to record voiceovers explaining each stage, creating a multimedia 'choose your own adventure' style experience for peers to explore.

Students create and perform simple digital puppet shows that illustrate the journey of a product from raw material to consumer, using characters and scenes to sequence each step of the production system. This hands-on storytelling activity helps Year 1 learners demonstrate their understanding of how systems prepare products in an engaging, age-appropriate way.

Students take photos with iPads of different products and their packaging, then use PicCollage to visually sort and label the stages of processing, transportation, and storage, supporting hands-on exploration of systems.

Classes watch age-appropriate videos from Clickview's 'Farm to Table' or 'War on Waste' series, then discuss and document the systems involved in preparing products for consumers, reinforcing real-world connections.

Children animate photos of products (like apples or milk cartons) using ChatterPix Kids, giving each product a 'voice' to describe its journey through the system, supporting oral language and sequencing skills.

Students work in teams to design and build a miniature production line using recycled materials, cardboard, string, tubes, and small containers to represent each stage in a product's journey (e.g., farm, factory, delivery, shop, consumer, and recycling). They craft physical models for each stage, then use iPads and a simple stop-motion app (e.g., Stop Motion Studio) to animate a chosen product (like a clay apple or a cardboard toy car) moving through their handmade system. Students must problem-solve how to physically move their product from stage to stage (using pulleys, ramps, or levers they build), then digitally capture and narrate the process, demonstrating their understanding of how everyday items are organised and delivered to consumers.

plannerText: This unit explores the systems involved in preparing products for consumers, focusing on the journey from raw materials to finished goods. Students investigate and sequence the stages of production, processing, transportation, and waste, using hands-on activities, digital storytelling, and collaborative inquiry to understand how everyday items reach them.

ci: Weather affects our lives in different ways.
lo: Types of weather conditions; How weather impacts our lives (clothing, activities, food, feelings); Why weather changes (seasons, patterns)

s.d: Students become 'weather reporters' by capturing daily weather changes using the iPad Camera, then app smash into Delightex to create interactive, animated weather scenes. They can drag and drop digital clothing, foods, and activities onto avatars to show how weather impacts daily life, then record their voices explaining their choices.

Students take photos of their own weather-related drawings or classroom weather charts, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with talking mouths. Each student records a short weather report or explains how the weather affects what they wear or do that day, building confidence and oral language skills.

Students create vibrant digital collages that showcase different weather types and how they affect daily life, combining photos, drawings, and text to represent clothing, activities, and foods for each season. This hands-on activity encourages them to visually organize their observations and express their understanding of weather?s impact in a fun, age-appropriate format.

Students create and answer simple weather surveys using Microsoft Forms to collect data about their classmates' favourite weather types and how weather affects their daily choices like clothing and activities. They then view the automatic charts to discuss which weather conditions impact their lives most and reflect on seasonal changes, making the data collection and analysis concrete and interactive.

Students create simple animated stories in ScratchJr showing how weather changes affect their day. For example, they can program a character to choose an umbrella when it rains or put on a hat when it is sunny, reinforcing cause and effect through coding and storytelling.

Students use the Design Cycle to invent and build wearable weather accessories that respond to different weather conditions. First, they explore weather needs (e.g., sun, rain, wind, cold) and brainstorm wearable solutions (like hats, badges, or capes). Using recycled materials, felt, cardboard, and craft supplies, they prototype their designs?such as a rain-detecting badge or a sun-protection visor. Next, they integrate tech by adding a simple programmable component: students use Sphero Indi color tiles as 'weather sensors' placed around the classroom. When an Indi drives over a certain color (representing a weather type), it triggers the student to put on the matching wearable and explain its purpose. Students test, iterate, and visually document their process. The project culminates in a 'Weather Wearables Parade,' where students showcase their inventions and demonstrate how their creations help people adapt to changing weather.

plannerText: The unit engages Year 1 students in exploring how weather affects daily life, including clothing, activities, and food choices. Students investigate weather patterns, collect and graph data, compare seasonal changes, and express their understanding through creative activities such as weather reports, tableaux, and music. The unit emphasizes student agency, hands-on inquiry, and visual documentation of learning.

ci: Living things have structures that allow them to survive in different environments.
lo: The physical structure of plants and bees; How features support survival in different environments; Human impact on living things

s.d: Launch an immersive 'Plant & Bee Adaptation AR Gallery' where students use the iPad Camera to photograph their own bean plants and bee sculptures, then upload these into Delightex to create interactive, explorable 3D dioramas showing how living things adapt to different environments. Students can add voice notes explaining each adaptation, making their learning visible and shareable in a truly innovative way.

Students create talking plants or bees by drawing or photographing their chosen living thing, then using ChatterPix Kids to animate it with a voiceover explaining how its structures help it survive. This brings scientific concepts to life in a playful, memorable format.

Students create a simple video diary using iMovie to document the growth and changes in their broad bean plants. They record short clips or photos of their plants in different conditions, add voice recordings explaining how the plant?s features help it survive, and combine these elements into a visual story that highlights the impact of environmental factors on plant survival.

Combine Seesaw with the iPad Camera and drawing tools to create a 'Plant and Animal Adaptation Detective' activity. Students photograph different plants and animals around the school, annotate the images with arrows and labels, and record voice explanations about the adaptations they observe.

Students make vibrant visual collages comparing the structures of different living things (e.g., leaves, stems, animal features) and how these help them survive in their environments. They can use stickers, cut-outs, and their own photos to create side-by-side adaptation comparisons.

s.t: Adaptation Obstacle Course: Animal Survival Challenge
s.d: Students work in teams to design and build a hands-on obstacle course that represents different environmental challenges (e.g., rivers, deserts, forests) using cardboard, popsticks, recycled materials, and clay. Each team chooses or creates a model animal (e.g., using clay, pipe cleaners, or recycled items) and researches its unique adaptations for survival. Integrating Sphero Indi robots as 'animals', students physically attach their crafted adaptations (like wings, fins, or camouflage coverings) onto the robots or models. Using the Indi's color tile coding, students program their animal to navigate the course, demonstrating how specific adaptations help them overcome obstacles. The project follows the Design Cycle: students investigate animal needs, ideate and prototype their obstacles and adaptations, test and refine both the course and their animal's design, and finally document their process with photos and voice reflections. This project fuses creative building, hands-on science, and digital coding, making adaptations and survival tangible and interactive.

plannerText: Students explore the structures and adaptations of living things through hands-on activities like growing broad beans in different conditions, scientific drawing, and clay bee models. They investigate plant and animal adaptations, document growth, and reflect on how environmental factors influence survival, with strong emphasis on visual learning, student agency, and creative expression.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: History and culture connect the past, present and future.
lo: How technology has changed over time; The purpose and significance of artefacts; How the past connects to the present and future

s.d: Launch a collaborative virtual 'Museum of Time' where students upload photos of family artefacts, record short voice stories about their significance, and co-create a digital timeline that visually connects their personal histories to broader changes in technology, dance, and daily life.

Students take photos of old and new artefacts (e.g., phones, toys, dance shoes) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, giving each artefact a 'voice' to tell its story from the past, present, or imagined future.

Students use Google Maps to trace family origins and migration stories, dropping pins on ancestral locations and mapping journeys across time. They compare historical and present-day maps to visualise how culture and technology have changed the way we connect to place. This activity makes history and culture tangible, fostering personal connections to the past, present, and future.

Learners explore digital books about history and culture on Epic, choosing a story that connects the past to the present. They summarise their chosen story and share how it relates to their own family or community history.

Students create a colorful digital timeline in PicCollage by arranging photos of artefacts, family tradition drawings, and images of evolving technology. They add simple captions and stickers to show how history and culture connect the past, present, and future, making their timelines visually engaging and easy to share with the class.

Students use the Design Cycle to create a 'Family Time Capsule Museum' exhibit. Each child selects a family tradition or artefact (e.g., a recipe, musical instrument, special object) and builds a 3D model of it using cardboard, clay, recycled materials, and craft supplies. Next, students use iPads to record a short video explanation or interview about the artefact's history and importance, then generate a QR code linking to their video. The QR code is attached to their physical model. Students collaboratively arrange their models into a 'museum' timeline, adding signs and decorations. Finally, they design and build a 'future artefact'?a creative invention predicting how this tradition or object might look or work in 50 years?using the same materials, and link it to a new digital recording. The hybrid museum is shared with families, blending hands-on making, storytelling, and digital technology.

plannerText: Students explore how history and culture connect the past, present, and future by investigating artefacts, creating timelines, comparing family traditions, and predicting future changes. Activities include sorting photos, discussing technological evolution, and designing future devices, with strong emphasis on visual, hands-on, and collaborative learning.

ci: We learn about ourselves through the games we play.
lo: How to identify and manage emotions; How players' choices affect relationships; Our responsibilities when playing games

s.d: Students collaboratively design a digital board game about playground fairness using Delightex, then bring their characters to life by recording voice-acted game scenarios in Puppet Pals. This app smash allows students to explore multiple perspectives and practice resolving conflict through creative storytelling.

Students document and reflect on their own game creations and playground experiences by capturing photos, drawing, and recording voice-overs in Seesaw. They can share strategies for fair play and problem-solving, building a digital portfolio of social learning moments.

Students take photos of their favorite playground games or team mascots, then animate them with their own voices using ChatterPix Kids to explain the rules, share tips for teamwork, or describe how to handle disagreements during play.

Students create visual collages of 'What Makes a Game Fair?' using photos from the iPad camera, stickers, and drawings. They can visually sort examples of positive and negative playground behaviors, then present their collages to the class for discussion.

Students create short digital movies where they act out and narrate the essential agreements for playing games fairly and respectfully. They use iMovie to record their voices and videos, then add simple titles and transitions to make their agreements come alive and feel personal. This hands-on storytelling helps them reflect on how their choices affect relationships and responsibilities during play.

Students work in groups to invent and build their own original tabletop game that teaches fairness, teamwork, and conflict resolution. Using recycled cardboard, popsticks, bottle caps, and craft materials, each group designs a unique game board and physical pieces. They use Bee-Bots or Sphero Indi robots as game movers, programming simple sequences to represent turns or challenges. Students collaboratively create the rules, decorate their games, and test them with classmates, iterating based on feedback. The process encourages discussion on fair play, making and following rules, and solving disagreements. The final games are showcased in a 'Play Fair Expo,' where groups explain how their game reflects positive social interactions and demonstrate how to play using both the physical and digital elements.

plannerText: This unit explores how students learn about themselves through the games they play, focusing on fairness, rules, teamwork, and resolving conflict. Activities include creating essential agreements, reflecting on emotions and behavior during play, building team identities, and using digital tools to document and share learning about positive social interactions.

ci: Artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures.
lo: Beliefs and values contribute to a culture; The importance of rituals and traditions; How artefacts capture history and cultural expression

s.d: Students create an interactive, virtual 'museum' of family artefacts and traditions using Delightex. They photograph their own artefacts or celebration items with the iPad Camera, then app smash into Delightex to design immersive, clickable exhibits that include audio explanations and digital drawings, allowing classmates to 'visit' and learn about each other's cultures in a playful, game-like environment.

Students photograph a personal or cultural artefact, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the artefact so it 'talks' and shares its story, beliefs, or cultural significance in the student's own voice. This brings artefacts to life and builds oral language skills while celebrating cultural diversity.

Students create vibrant digital collages showcasing artefacts, photos, and drawings of their family traditions and celebrations. They arrange images and add simple text labels to explain how these artefacts reflect their culture?s beliefs and values, then share their collages with classmates to celebrate diversity and spark conversations about cultural expression.

Students design vibrant digital posters in Canva about a chosen cultural celebration or tradition, using the 'Remove Background' feature to layer themselves into scenes. They then upload and narrate their posters in Seesaw, explaining the meaning behind the celebration and its artefacts.

Students create short animated skits in Puppet Pals, using characters and backgrounds inspired by their own or classmates' cultural stories and rituals. They script and record voiceovers to dramatize how artefacts are used in celebrations, fostering empathy and understanding of diverse perspectives.

Students work in teams to research and select a cultural celebration or tradition from around the world. Using cardboard, recycled materials, fabric, and clay, they design and build a handheld artefact or wearable accessory (e.g., mask, headdress, instrument) that symbolizes their chosen culture. Next, they use Makey Makey kits and conductive materials to embed simple digital sound triggers into their artefacts?each touch or movement plays a related sound, song, or greeting recorded on an iPad. Students parade their creations, sharing the story and meaning behind each artefact, and invite classmates to interact with the sound-embedded features, creating a multisensory, hands-on celebration of diversity.

plannerText: This unit explores how artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures. Students investigate their own and others' traditions, celebrations, and cultural expressions through artefacts, music, dance, and storytelling, culminating in creative projects that celebrate diversity and foster intercultural understanding.

ci: Technology can influence the way ecosystems can survive.
lo: An ecosystem has animals, plants and microorganisms; Technology can cause ecosystems to adapt and evolve; Technology can help or hinder ecosystems

s.d: Students create interactive 3D models of different ecosystems using Merge Cubes and the Merge Explorer app on iPads. They record short ChatterPix Kids voiceovers as 'ecosystem reporters' explaining how technology (like satellites or robots) is helping or harming their chosen ecosystem, then overlay these on their Merge Cube models for an immersive, hands-on AR experience.

Students document the evolution of technology in ecosystems by capturing photos or videos of their class 'robot habitats' and narrating their observations. They can use Seesaw's drawing and annotation tools to highlight how technology changes the balance or function of their mini-ecosystems.

Students code simple stories showing how a robot or piece of technology interacts with an ecosystem. For example, they might animate a bee-bot pollinating flowers or a robot cleaning up pollution, helping them visualise cause and effect in a playful, accessible way.

Students create vibrant digital collages that showcase how technology influences ecosystems by combining photos, drawings, and text. They visually compare natural habitats before and after technological changes, highlighting adaptations and impacts on plants and animals. This hands-on activity encourages creativity while deepening understanding of the central idea through storytelling and visual synthesis.

Students investigate local ecosystems by using the Field Guide to Victoria app to identify plants, animals, and microorganisms in their schoolyard or nearby park. They document how technology helps them observe and record living things, then create a digital nature journal reflecting on how technology supports ecosystem survival and adaptation.

s.t: Rescue Bot Rainforest: Adaptive Ecosystem Challenge
s.d: Students work in teams to design and construct a vibrant 3D rainforest ecosystem using recycled cardboard, fabric scraps, clay, and natural materials. They identify 'threats' (like deforestation or pollution) represented by physical obstacles or removable elements in their model. Using Sphero Indi robots (or Bee-Bots), students prototype and decorate custom 'rescue bot' shells from recycled materials.
